question:

The molecular mass of water ${\text{(}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}})$ is 18.02 u.
To calculate the molecular mass of a compound, the sum of the atomic masses of the constituent atoms is taken.
explanation: The molecular mass of water is correctly stated as 18.02 u, making Statement A true. Statement B is also true as molecular mass is indeed the sum of the atomic masses of all atoms in the molecule. Correct option is (1)

question: The average molecular weight of the air which has $21 \%$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $79 \%$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$, is
explanation:
Percentage of $\mathrm{N}_{2}=79 \%$
Percentage of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=21 \%$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{N}_{2}=28 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Average molecular weight
$ = \frac{{\left( {\% \,\,{{\text{N}}_2} \times {{\text{M}}_{{{\text{N}}_2}}}} \right) + \left( {\% \,{{\text{O}}_2} \times {{\text{M}}_{{{\text{O}}_2}}}} \right)}}{{100}}$
$ = \frac{{(79 \times 28) + (21 \times 32)}}{{100}} = \frac{{2212 + 672}}{{100}} = 28.84$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following statements is not true about atomic mass?
explanation: Atomic mass is a weighted average of all the isotopes of that element, in which the mass of each isotope is multiplied by the abundance of that particular isotope. It is also expressed relative to $\mathrm{C}^{12}$ isotope of carbon, also atomic mass of Na is 23 . Hence, option (3) not true and is the correct option.

Assertion: 1 amu equals to $1.66 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~g}$.


Reason: $1.66 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~g}$. equals to $\dfrac{1}{12}$ th of mass of a C-12 atom.
explanation: The mass of 1 atom of ${\text{C - 12}}$ is $1.66 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~g}$. and this value is used as the definition of an atomic mass unit  $(1\,{\text{amu}})$, which is equal to $1.66 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~g}$. So, the option (1) is correct

Reason: Vapour density $\times 2=$ Molecular weight.
explanation: This is the vapor density method for the determination of molecular weight. So, the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Atomic mass has no unit but expressed in amu.


Reason: It is the average mass of an atom taking care of relative abundance of all its possible isotopes.
explanation:
The average mass of an atom is determined by the weighted average of its isotopes' masses. It is calculated using the formula
Average atomic mass $=$
$\sum($ Relative Abundance $) \times($ Atomic mass of isotope)
This provides a dimensionless atomic mass that accounts for the relative abundances of isotopes in the element. So, the option (1) is correct.

explanation: A mass spectrometer determines atomic or molecular weight by ionizing a sample and utilizing magnetic and electric fields to separate ions based on their mass-to-charge ratios,enabling precise measurement of atomic weights and isotopic identification. So, the option (1) is correct.

question: Neon has two isotopes $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{20}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{22}}$. If atomic weight of Neon is 20.2 , the ratio of the relative abundances of the isotopes is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{\mathrm{Wt} \%}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{20}=\mathrm{x}}$ $\mathrm{\mathrm{Wt} \%}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{22}=100-\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{20.2=\dfrac{[\mathrm{x} \times 20]+[(100-\mathrm{x}) 22]}{100} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=90 \%}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{wt} \%}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{20}=90 \%}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{wt} \%}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{22}=100-90=10 \%}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Ne}^{20}: \mathrm{Ne}^{22}=90: 10=9: 1}$

question: The vapour density of gas $\rm{{\text{A}}}$ is four times that of $\rm{{\text{B}}}$. If molecular mass of $\rm{{\text{B}}}$ is $\rm{{\text{M}}}$, then molecular mass of $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{V} . \mathrm{D} \times 2=}$ molar mass
when V.D. of A is 4 times to V.D. of B, molar mass will also be 4 times i.e. $\mathrm{4 \mathrm{M}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{5.6 \mathrm{~L}}$ of a gas at S.T.P. weighs equal to $\mathrm{8 \mathrm{~g}}$. The vapour density of gas is
explanation:
Mass of $\mathrm{22.4 \mathrm{~L}}$ gas at S.T.P. $\mathrm{=\dfrac{8 \times 22.4}{5.6}=}$ $\mathrm{32 \mathrm{~g}(1}$ mole $\mathrm{)}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Mol. mass $\mathrm{=32 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mole}}$
Vapour density is half of molar mass
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ V.D $\mathrm{=\dfrac{32}{2}=16}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour of gas A is three times that of gas B. If the molecular weight of A is M, the molecular weight of B is
explanation:
$\frac{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{B}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{d}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\rm{d}}_{\rm{B}}}}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{\rm{d = vapour}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{density}}} \right)$
${\rm{Given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{d}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = 3}}{{\rm{d}}_{{\rm{B,}}}}\frac{{{\rm{dA}}}}{{{\rm{dB}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{B}}}}}{\rm{ = 3}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}$
${{\rm{M}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{M}}}{{\rm{3}}}$

${\rm{ = 2 \times 66}}$
${\rm{ = 136}}$

question: The sulphate of a metal M contains 9.87% of M. This sulphate is isomorphous with ${\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$. The atomic weight of M is
explanation:
As the given sulphate is isomorphous with ${\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$, its formula would be ${\rm{MS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$. If m is the atomic weight of M, molecular weight of ${\rm{MS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O = m + 32 + 64 + 126 = m + 222}}{\rm{.}}$
Hence, ${\rm{\% }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M = }}\frac{{\rm{m}}}{{{\rm{m + 222}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 9}}{\rm{.87}}$ (Given)
or ${\rm{100m = 9}}{\rm{.87m + 222 \times 9}}{\rm{.87}}$
or ${\rm{90}}{\rm{.13m = 222 \times 9}}{\rm{.87}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{m = 24}}{\rm{.3}}$.

question: Dalton's atomic theory could not explain which of the following?
explanation: Dalton's atomic theory focuses on the mass relationships in chemical reactions. The law of gaseous volumes deals with volume relationships, which Dalton's theory does not address. Therefore, Dalton's atomic theory could not explain the law of gaseous volume.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
According to Dalton's atomic theory compounds are formed when atoms of different elements combine in a fixed ratio.
According to Dalton's atomic theory, atoms can be neither created nor destroyed, and the same type of atoms can combine.
explanation: Statement  A  is a true reflection of Dalton's atomic theory, which states that compounds are the result of different atoms combining in a specific ratio. However, Statement B is false because, according to Dalton's theory, atoms of the same kind cannot combine with each other; this is a misconception that was later corrected with the discovery of diatomic molecules and polyatomic elements. Therefore, while Statement A is true, Statement B is not true based on Dalton's original atomic theory. Correct option is (2)

Atoms of a given element are identical in size, mass, and chemical properties.
Atoms are indivisible particles that cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
explanation: According to Dalton's atomic theory, atoms of a given element are indeed identical in size, mass, and other properties.    Additionally, Dalton's postulates also state that atoms are indivisible in a chemical process, reinforcing the law of conservation of mass. Hence, both statements A and B are true as per Dalton's atomic theory. Correct option is (1)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
An empirical formula represents the exact number of different types of atoms present in a molecule of a compound.
A molecular formula may be the same as or a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula.
explanation: Statement A is false because the empirical formula represents the simplest ratio of the elements in a compound, not the exact number. Statement B is true because a molecular formula can be a multiple of the empirical formula, reflecting the actual number of atoms in a molecule. Correct option is (3)

question: The empirical formula of a compound is $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}$ and its vapour density is 45 . What is the molecular formula of the compound?
explanation:
Empirical formula $=\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}$
Empirical formula mass $=2(12)+5(1)+16$ $=45$
Vapour density (V. D.) $=45$.
So, molar mass $=2 \times$ V.D. $=2 \times 45=90$
$\mathrm{n}=\dfrac{\text { molar mass }}{\text { E.F. mass }}=\dfrac{90}{45}=2$
M.F. $=(\text { E.F. })_{\mathrm{n}}=\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}\right)_{2}=\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: An organic compound has $42.1 \%$ carbon, $6.4 \%$ hydrogen and remainder is oxygen. If its molecular weight is 342 , then its molecular formula is
explanation:
Carbon $42.1 \%$ by mass means 100 g compound has 42.1 g carbon
So, 342 gram compound contains
$\dfrac{342 \times 42.1}{100}=144 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon
$\therefore$ No. of carbon atoms $=\dfrac{144}{12}=12$
Similarly, mass of hydrogen
$=\dfrac{342 \times 6.4}{100}=22 \mathrm{~g} \text { hydrogen }$
$\therefore$ No. of hydrogen atoms $=\dfrac{22}{1}=22$
And mass of oxygen$=\dfrac{342 \times 51.5}{100}=176 \mathrm{~g} \text { oxygen }$
$\therefore$ No. of oxygen atoms $=\dfrac{176}{16}=11$
So, the formula is $\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}$
The correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry

question:
An organic compound containing oxygen, carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen contains ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ carbon, ${\mathrm{6.7 \%}}$ hydrogen and ${\mathrm{46.67 \%}}$ nitrogen. Its molecular mass was found to be ${\mathrm{60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$. The total number of atoms in the molecular formula of the compound is ____ .
(At. wt. : ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}=12 ; \mathrm{H}=1 ; \mathrm{N}=14 ; \mathrm{O}=16}}$ )
explanation:
Sum of ${\mathrm{\%=20+6.7+46.67=73.37}}$
This is less than ${\mathrm{100 \%}}$. Hence, compound contains adequate oxygen so that the total percentage of elements is ${\mathrm{100 \%}}$.
Hence, ${\mathrm{\%}}$ of oxygen ${\mathrm{=100-73.37}}$$=26.63 \%$
Moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}=\dfrac{\% \text { of } \mathrm{C}}{\text { Atomic mass of } \mathrm{C}}=\dfrac{20}{12}}}$$=1.667 \mathrm{~mol}$
Moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}=\dfrac{\% \text { of } \mathrm{H}}{\text { Atomic mass of } \mathrm{H}}=\dfrac{6.7}{1.0}}}$ ${\mathrm{=6.700 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
Moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{\% \text { of } \mathrm{N}}{\text { Atomic mass of } \mathrm{N}}=\dfrac{46.67}{14}}}$ ${\mathrm{=3.334 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
Moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}=\dfrac{\% \text { of } \mathrm{O}}{\text { Atomic mass of } \mathrm{O}}=\dfrac{26.63}{16}}}$${\mathrm{=1.664 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
$\therefore $${\mathrm{\dfrac{1.667}{1.664} \approx 1, \dfrac{6.700}{1.664}=4, \dfrac{3.334}{1.664}=2}}$, and ${\mathrm{\dfrac{1.664}{1.664}=1}}$
Hence, the ratio of number of moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{N}: \mathrm{O}}}$ is ${\mathrm{1: 4: 2: 1}}$.
$\therefore $ Empirical formula ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{CH}_{4} \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Empirical formula mass ${\mathrm{=12+4+28+16}}$$=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Hence,
Molecular formula ${\mathrm{=}}$ Empirical formula ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{CH}_{4} \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
Thus, total number of atoms in its molecular formula is 8 .
subject: Chemistry

question:
2.4 g of sample of magnesium when burnt in excess of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ gives 3.84 g of magnesium oxide. The percentage purity of magnesium in the sample is ____ ${\mathrm{\%}}$.
[Molar mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{MgO}=40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ ]
explanation:
The reaction is
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}}}$
Moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}=\dfrac{2.4}{24}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}=}}$ Moles of MgO
Theoretical yield of MgO
${\mathrm{=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \times 40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=4.0 \mathrm{~g}}}$
Practical yield of MgO is 3.84 g
$\therefore $ Percentage purity ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{3.84}{4.0} \times 100=96 \%}}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation:
Molecular mass of metal chloride
${\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.57}{100} \times 22400=127.68}}$
Mass of chloride in metal chloride
${\mathrm{=127.68 \times \dfrac{55}{100}=70 \mathrm{~g}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Moles of chlorine in the given compound
${\mathrm{=\dfrac{70}{35.5}=2}}$
So, formula of metal chloride ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{MCl}_{2}}}$

Mass of other component
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.33 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 23}}}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.66 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 24}}}}}}{\rm{ = 14}}{\rm{.04 = 135}}{\rm{.04}}\,\,{\rm{amu}}$
$\therefore $ Total mass of one molecule
= 108 + 13 + 14.04 = 135.04 amu
$\therefore $ Mol. mass of substance = 135.04

question: A gaseous compound of nitrogen and hydrogen contains 12.5 % by mass of hydrogen. The density of the compound relative to hydrogen is 16. The molecular formula of the compound is:

$Mol.wt. = 16 \times 2 = 32$
Molecular formula $ = n \times empirical\,\,formula\,\,mass$
$n = \frac{{32}}{{16}} = 2$

${\rm{Percentage}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{O = 100 - (40 + 6}}{\rm{.6) = 53}}{\rm{.4\% }}$
Hence, empirical formula ${\rm{ = C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
${\rm{n = }}\frac{{{\rm{Molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{Empirical}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{formula}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{60}}}}{{{\rm{30}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}$

question: Match the following laws with their correct descriptions. Choose the correct option:
explanation:

(B - R): The law of definite proportions, presented by Joseph Proust, suggests that a given compound always contains exactly the same proportion of elements by weight.
(C - S): The law of multiple proportions, proposed by Dalton, indicates that if two elements combine to form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in the ratio of small whole numbers.
(D - Q): Gay Lussac's law of gaseous volumes states that when gases combine or are produced in a chemical reaction, they do so in a simple ratio by volume at the same temperature and pressure. Correct option is (1)

question: Copper sulphate pentahydrate contains ${\mathrm{25.45 \%}}$ of copper and ${\mathrm{36.08 \%}}$ of water. If the law of constant proportions is true, then the weight of copper required to produce 20 g of copper sulphate pentahydrate will be ____ g.
explanation:
100 g of copper sulphate pentahydrate is obtained from 25.45 g of copper.
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 1 \mathrm{~g}}}$ of copper sulphate pentahydrate will be obtained from ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{25.45}{100} \mathrm{~g}}}$ of copper.
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 20 \mathrm{~g}}}$ of copper sulphate pentahydrate will be obtained from ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{25.45}{100} \times 20=5.09 \mathrm{~g}}}$ of copper.
$ \approx 5\,{\rm{g}}\,$ of copper.

question: Which of the following statements indicates that law of multiple proporiton is being followed?
explanation:
The element, carbon, combines with oxygen to form two compounds namely, carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide. In ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$, 12 parts by mass of carbon combine with 32 parts by mass of oxygen while in CO, 12 parts by mass of carbon combine with 16 parts by mass of oxygen.
Therefore, the masses of oxygen combine with a fixed mass of carbon (12 parts) in ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and CO are 32 and 16 respectively. These masses of oxygen bear a simple ratio of 32 : 16 or 2 : 1 to each other.
This is an example of law of multiple proportion.

question: ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}$ contains 5.88% hydrogen, ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$ contains 11.11% hydrogen while ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$  contains 50% sulphur. These figures illustrate the
explanation:
In ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}$,
Mass of hydrogen = 5.88 g
Mass of sulphur = 100 – 5.88 = 94.12 g
In ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$,
Mass of hydrogen = 11.11 g
Mass of oxygen = 100 – 11.11 = 88.89 g
11.11 g of hydrogen combines with 88.89 g of oxygen
5.88 g of hydrogen combines with
$\frac{{{\rm{88}}{\rm{.89 \times 5}}{\rm{.88}}}}{{{\rm{11}}{\rm{.11}}}}{\rm{ = 47}}{\rm{.04}}$ g of oxygen.
The ratio of masses of sulphur and oxygen which combine with fixed amount of hydrogen is
94.12 : 47.04 = 2 : 1
In ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$,
Mass of sulphur = 50 g
Mass of oxygen = 100 – 50 = 50 g
The ration of masses of sulphur and oxygen = 50 : 50 = 1 : 1
From eq. (1) and (2),
The ratio of sulphur and oxygen = 2 : 1
Hence, the given data follows Law of reciprocal proportion.

A sample of air increases in volume when heated at constant pressure but its mass remains unaltered
The weight of a piece of platinum is the same before and after heating in air

subject: Chemistry

question: 3 g of a hydrocarbon on combustion in excess of oxygen produces 8.8 g of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 5.4 g of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$. The data illustrates the law of
explanation:
Mass of carbon in ${\rm{8}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 12 \times }}\frac{{{\rm{8}}{\rm{.8}}}}{{{\rm{44}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.4}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
Mass of hydrogen in ${\rm{5}}{\rm{.4}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O = 2 \times }}\frac{{{\rm{5}}{\rm{.4}}}}{{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.6}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
Total mass of (C + H) = 2.4 + 0.6 = 3.0 g
Hence, obey’s law of conservation of mass.
subject: Chemistry

question: Two samples of lead oxide were separately reduced to metallic lead by heating in a current of hydrogen. The weight of lead from one oxide was half the weight of lead obtained from the other oxide. The data illustrates
explanation: This shows that the weights of lead combining with fixed weight of oxygen are in the ratio $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{:1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{1:2}}$ which is in accordance with law of multiple proportions.
subject: Chemistry

question: In the reaction, ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{2N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$, the ratio of volumes of nitrogen, hydrogen and ammonia is 1 : 3 : 2. These figures illustrate the law of
explanation: Gay Lussac’s Law of Combining Volumes states that when gases react, they do so in volumes which bear a simple ratio to one another, and to the volume of the product(s) formed if gaseous, at constant temperature and pressure.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gay Lussaac's Law of Gaseous Volumes

question: In compound A, 1.0 g nitrogen combines with 0.57 g oxygen. In compound B, 2.0 g nitrogen unite with 2.24 g oxygen and in compound C, 3.0 g nitrogen combine with 5.11 g oxygen. These results obey the law of:
explanation:
Given that,
Compound A, 1 g of Nitrogen combine with 0.57 of Oxygen
Compound B, 2 g of Nitrogen combine with 2.24 g of Oxygen
1 g of Nitrogen combine with ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1 \times 2}}{\rm{.24}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.12g}}$ of Oxygen
Compound C, 3 g of Nitrogen combine with 5.11 g of Oxygen
1 g of Nitrogen combine with ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1 \times 5}}{\rm{.11}}}}{{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.70g}}$of Oxygen
The ratio of Oxygen in A, B, and C = 0.57 : 1.12 : 1.7 = 1 : 2 : 3.
Therefore, given results obey law of multiple proportions.
subject: Chemistry

question: Irrespective of the source, pure sample of water always yields 88.89% mass of oxygen and 11.11% mass of hydrogen. This is explained by the law of
explanation: ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$  contains H and O in a fixed ratio by mass. It illustrates law of constant composition.
subject: Chemistry

question: The law of multiple proportions was proposed by
explanation: Law of multiple proportions was proposed by Dalton.
subject: Chemistry

question: In an experiment, ${\rm{5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{CaC}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ on heating gave ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{CaO}}$ and ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$. The results are in accordance with the
explanation:
Mass of ${\rm{CaC}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{CaO + Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
${\rm{5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g = 2}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ + 2}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g = 5g}}$
Hence, given data obey’s law of conservation of mass
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following statements best explains the law of conservation of mass?
explanation:
According to Law of conservation of mass,
Total mass of reactants = Total mass of products

options.text: 100 g water is heated to give steam.
A sample of ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ gas is heated at constant pressure without any change in mass.
36 g of carbon combines with 32 g of oxygen to form 68 g of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$.
10 g of carbon is heated in vacuum without any change in mass.

subject: Chemistry

question: Two elements X (Z = 16) and Y (Z = 14) combine to form compounds A, B and C. The ratio of different masses of Y which combine with a fixed mass of X in A, B and C is 1 : 3 : 5. If 32 parts by mass of X combines with 84 parts by mass of Y in B, then in C, 16 parts by mass of X will combine with
explanation: The ratio of different masses of Y which combines with a fixed mass of X in A, B and C is 1 : 3 : 5.32 parts by mass of X combines with 84 parts by mass of Y to form B. Hence, 16 parts of the mass of X combine with 42 parts by mass of Y. The ratio of Y in B & C is 3 : 5.16 parts by mass of X combines with ${\rm{42 \times }}\frac{{\rm{5}}}{{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 70}}$ parts by mass of Y to form C.
subject: Chemistry

question: Zinc sulphate contains 22.65% of zinc and 43.9% of water of crystallisation. If the law of constant proportions is true, then the weight of zinc required to produce 20 g of the crystals will be
explanation:
To prepare 20 g of the crystals, zinc required
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{22}}{\rm{.65}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ \times 20 = 4}}{\rm{.53g}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following represents Avogadro’s hypothesis?
explanation:
According to Avogadro's law,
Equal volumes of all gases contain equal number of molecules under same condition of temperature and pressure

options.text: Equal volumes of all gases under same condition of temperature and pressure contain equal number of atoms
Equal volumes of all gases under same condition of temperature and pressure contain equal number of molecules
Gases react together in volumes which bear a simple ratio to one another
The rates of diffusion of gases are inversely proportional to the square root of their densities

subject: Chemistry

question:
Equal moles of different substances contain same number of constituent particles.
Equal weights of different substances contain the same number of constituent particles.
explanation: Equal moles of different substances contain same number of constituent particles but equal weights of different substances do not contain the same number of constituent particles.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Volume of a gas is inversely proportional to the number of moles of gas.
The ratio by volume of gaseous reactants and products is in agreement with their mole ratio.
explanation: We know that from the reaction ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{2HCl}}$ that the ratio of the volume of gaseous reactants and products is in agreement with their molar ratio. The ratio of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{:C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{:HCl}}$ volume is 1: 1: 2 which is the same as their molar ratio. Thus volume of gas is directly related to the number of moles. Therefore, Statement A is incorrect but statement B is correct

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry

Under identical conditions 1 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 1 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ gas contain the same number of moles
1 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 1 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ gas contain the same number of oxygen atoms
explanation: 1 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 1L : of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ contain the same number of moles and hence number of molecules but not atoms. number of atoms in ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ will be 1.5 times than that of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

Pure water obtained from different sources such as river, well, spring etc., always contains hydrogen and oxygen combined in same ratio 1 : 8 by mass.

question: One of the following combination which illustrates the law of reciprocal proportions?
explanation: In law of reciprocal proportions, the two elements combining with the third element, must combine with each other in the same ratio or multiple of that ratio S and O when combine with C is 2 : 1. Ratio of S and O is ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is 1:1.

question: ${\rm{10}}\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$  gas and ${\rm{10}}\,\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of gas x at the same temperature contain the same number of molecules, the gas X is
explanation:
The number of molecules of ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and X are same. Hence they must have the same molecular weights.
$\therefore {\rm{X}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{is}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{CO}}$.

question: The percentage of copper and oxygen in samples of CuO obtained by different methods were found to be the same. This illustrates the law of
explanation: According to law of constant proportions a pure chemical compound always contains same elements combined together in the same definite proportion of weight.

question: The combining ratios of hydrogen and oxygen in water and hydrogen peroxide are 1 : 8 and 1 : 16. Which law is illustrated in this example?
explanation: The combining ratios of hydrogen and oxygen in water and hydrogen peroxide are 1: 8 and 1: 16. It is an example of law of multiple proportions. According to this law, if two elements combine together to form several compounds then weight of one of these elements, which combines with a fixed weight of the other, are in ratio of simple whole numbers.

options.text: Gay Lussac’s law of combining volumes of gases

question: If $3.01 \times 10^{20}$ molecules are removed from 98 mg of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, then the number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ left are
explanation:
No. of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in 98 mg
$=\dfrac{98 \times 10^{-3}}{98}=1 \times 10^{-3}$
No. of moles of $3.01 \times 10^{20}$ molecules of
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\dfrac{3.01 \times 10^{20}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3}$
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ left $=\left(1 \times 10^{-3}-0.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)$
$=0.5 \times 10^{-3}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What amount of dioxygen (in gram) contains $1.8 \times 10^{22}$ molecules?
explanation:
$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules are present in 32 g of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$.
$\therefore 1.8 \times 10^{22}$ molecules will be present in
$\dfrac{32}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \times 1.8 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~g}=0.960 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The molar mass of carbon dioxide is the sum of the atomic masses of two oxygen atoms and one carbon atom.
The molecular mass of a compound is determined by the weight ratio of the elements present in the compound.
explanation: Statement A is true because the molecular mass of carbon dioxide  ${\text{(C}}{{\text{O}}_2})$  is calculated by adding the atomic masses of one carbon atom (12.01 amu) and two oxygen atoms   $(2 \times 16.00\,\,{\text{amu}}.)$  Statement B is false as the molecular mass is based on the sum of the atomic masses of the elements in a molecule, not the weight ratio. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The mole concept is not applicable to particles as large as grains of sand.
The mole is a unit that can only be used to measure submicroscopic particles.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because the mole concept is typically used for submicroscopic particles where counting individual entities is not feasible. Statement B is false because the mole is a unit of measure that can be applied to count any discrete entities, regardless of their size. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The mass of $6.022 \times {10^{23}}$ molecules of glucose $({{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_6})$ is 180.16 grams.
Glucose has a molar mass equal to its molecular mass expressed in grams.
explanation: Statement A is true because the molecular mass of glucose is approximately 180.16 u , and one mole  $(6.022 \times {10^{23}}$  molecules) of glucose would therefore have a mass of 180.16 grams. Statement B is true as the molar mass of a substance is numerically equal to its molecular mass when the molecular mass is expressed in grams per mole.  Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Avogadro's number is the number of particles in exactly 12 g of carbon-12.
The value of Avogadro's number is based on the definition of the mole.
explanation: Statement A is true because by definition, Avogadro's number  $(6.02214076 \times {10^{23}})$  is the number of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12, which is the isotope used to define the mole. Statement B is also true as Avogadro's number is inherently linked to the definition of the mole, which is the amount of substance that contains as many entities as there are atoms in 12 g of carbon 12.   Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A mole of carbon dioxide ${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}$ has a mass of $44.01\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}.$
The number of atoms in a mole of carbon dioxide is equal to twice the Avogadro constant.
explanation: Statement A is true because the molar mass of carbon dioxide is the sum of the molar mass of one carbon atom  $(12.01\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}})$  and two oxygen atoms  $(2 \times 16.00\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol)}},$  which equals approximately   $44.01\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}.$  Statement B is false because a mole of carbon dioxide contains  $6.02214076 \times {10^{23}}$  molecules, each composed  of three atoms (one carbon and two oxygen), resulting in three times the Avogadro constant number of atoms in total, not twice.   Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The mass of one mole of sodium chloride ( NaCl ) is 58.5 grams.
The mole concept is only applicable to substances that exist as discrete molecules.
explanation: Statement A is true as the molar mass of sodium chloride is calculated by adding the atomic mass of sodium   $(22.99\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}})$   and chlorine  $(35.45\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol)}},$  which equals approximately  $58.5\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}.$  Statement B is false because the mole concept applies to any substance, including ionic compounds like sodium chloride, where it represents  $(6.02214076 \times {10^{23}})$  formula units. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The molar mass of water ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$ is $18.02\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}.$
One mole of water contains the same number of molecules as the Avogadro constant.
explanation: Statement A is true as the molar mass of water is calculated by adding the molar masses of hydrogen  $2 \times 1.008\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}$  and oxygen  $16.00\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}},$  which equals approximately  $18.02\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}.$  Statement B is also true as one mole of any substance, including water, contains  $6.02214076 \times {10^{23}}$  molecules, the Avogadro constant. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

A mole of any substance contains $6.022 \times {10^{23}}$ particles of that substance.
The number of particles in a mole is known as Avogadro's number.
explanation: Statement A is  true ;  a mole contains Avogadro's number of particles, which is  $6.022 \times {10^{23}}.$  Statement B is also  true ;   this number is indeed known as Avogadro's number. Correct option is (1)

explanation: The assertion correctly identifies the role of mole in linking the microscopic and macroscopic worlds. The reason correctly identifies the molar mass as the necessary conversion factor, explaining the assertion. Correct option is (1)

explanation: The molar mass is indeed the mass of one mole of a substance and is numerically equal to the substance average atomic or molecular mass when expressed in atomic mass units. explanation: The mole is indeed the unit used to count entities at the microscopic level, such as atoms, molecules, and ions. question: Which one of the following has the same number of atoms as are in 6g of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ?
explanation:
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\dfrac{6}{18}=0.33 \mathrm{~mol}$
Number of atoms in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ $=0.33 \times 3 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms $=0.99 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms $\approx 1 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms
(1) Moles of $\mathrm{He}=\dfrac{0.4}{4}=0.1$ moles
No. of atoms $=0.1 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms
(2) Moles of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}=\dfrac{22}{44}=0.5$ moles
No. of atoms $=0.5 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 3=1.5 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms
(3) Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{moles}$
No. of atoms $=\left(0.5 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}\right) \times 2=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms
(4) Moles of $\mathrm{CO}=\dfrac{12}{28}=0.42$ moles
No. of atoms $=0.42 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 2$
Therefore, 1 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ has the same number of atoms as are of 6 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of grams of oxygen in 32.2 g of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is approximately
explanation:
Molar mass of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ $=322 \mathrm{~g} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$322 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has 224 g oxygen
$32.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has
$=\dfrac{32.2 \times 224}{322}=22.4 \mathrm{~g}$ oxygen.
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of sodium ions present in 0.5 mole of sodium ferrocyanide is
explanation:
The number of sodium atoms in 1 mole of sodium ferrocyanide, $\mathrm{Na}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$
$=4 \times 6.0 \times 10^{23}$.
The number of sodium atoms in 0.5 moles
$=4 \times 0.5 \times 6.0 \times 10^{23}=12 \times 10^{23}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the total number of electrons present in one mole of ethane?
explanation:
1 molecule of ethane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)$ has $(6 \times 2)+$ $(1 \times 6)=18$ electrons.
So, 1 mole ethane will have
$6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 18=108.396 \times 10^{23}$ electrons.
subject: Chemistry

question: A spherical ball of radius 7cm contains $56 \%$ by mass iron. Find out the number of moles of iron present approximately in the ball.( $\mathrm{d}=1.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$; atomic mass $=56 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Given radius $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore$ Volume $=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}=\dfrac{4}{3} \times 3.14 \times(7)^{3}=1436 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
$\mathrm{n}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d} \times 10 \times \text { mass } \% \times \text { volume }}{\text { atomic mass } \times 1000}$
$\mathrm{n}=\dfrac{1.4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3} \times 10 \times 56 \times 1436 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}{56 \times 1000}$
$\mathrm{n}=20.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Hence, option (2) is correct.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Among the following, choose the ones with equal number of atoms.
A. 212 g of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})$ [molar mass $=106 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
B. 248 g of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$ [molar mass $=62 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
C. 240 g of $\mathrm{NaOH}(\mathrm{s})$ [molar mass $=40 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
D. 12 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ [molar mass $=2 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
E. 220 g of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ [molar mass $=44 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
Number of atoms $=\dfrac{\text { Given mass }}{\text { molar mass }} \times$ Atomicity $\times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
A. $\dfrac{212}{106} \times 6 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=12 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
B. $\dfrac{248}{62} \times 3 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=12 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
C. $\dfrac{240}{40} \times 3 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=18 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
D. $\dfrac{12}{2} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 2=12 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
E. $\dfrac{220}{44} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 3=15 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
So, A, B and D have same number of atoms.
subject: Chemistry

question:
$2.8 \times 10^{-3}$ mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ are left after removing $10^{21}$ molecules from its “x” mg sample. The mass of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ taken initially is
[Given: $\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.02 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-}$]
explanation:
${({\text{moles}})_{{\text{initial}}}} = \frac{{{\text{x}} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{44}}$

$(\text { moles })_{\text {removal }}=\dfrac{10^{21}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
$(\text { moles })_{\text {left }}=(\text { moles })_{\text {initial }}-(\text { moles })_{\text {removed }}$.

$2.8 \times {10^{ - 3}} = \frac{{{\text{x}} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{44}} - \frac{{{{10}^{21}}}}{{6.02 \times {{10}^{23}}}}$
$\mathrm{x}=196.2 \mathrm{mg}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A 19.6 g of a given gaseous sample contains 2.8 g of molecules $\left( {{\rm{d  = }}\,{\rm{0}}.{\rm{75}}\,{\rm{g\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right),{\rm{11}}.{\rm{2}}\,{\rm{g}}$ of molecules $\left( {{\rm{d}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{3}}\,{\rm{g\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$ and 5.6 g of molecules $({\rm{d}}\,{\rm{ = 1}}.{\rm{5}}\,{\rm{g\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}})$. All density measurements are made at STP. Calculate the total number of molecules ( ${\mathrm{N}}$ ) present in the given sample. Report your answer in ' ${\mathrm{10^{23} \mathrm{~N}}}$'. Assume Avogadro's number as ${\mathrm{6 \times 10^{23}}}$.
explanation:
Total weight ${\mathrm{=19.6 \mathrm{~g}}}$
(a) 2.8 g of molecules, ${\mathrm{d=0.765 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$
${{\rm{Volume}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}}}{{\rm{d}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.8}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.75}}}}\,{\rm{L}}\,\,{\rm{at}}\,{\rm{STP}}}$
${1\,{\rm{mol}} \equiv 22.4\,{\rm{L}}\,{\rm{at}}\,\,{\rm{STP}}}$
${{\rm{moles}} = \frac{{2.8}}{{0.75}} \times \frac{1}{{22.4}}}$
${{\rm{Molecules}} = \frac{{2.8}}{{0.75}} \times \frac{1}{{22.4}} \times 6 \times {{10}^{23}}}$
${\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 1 \times {{10}^{23}}}$
(b) 11.2 g molecules, ${\mathrm{d=3 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$
Molecules ${\mathrm{=1 \times 10^{23}}}$
(c) 5.6 g molecules, ${\mathrm{d=1.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}}$
Molecules ${\mathrm{=1 \times 10^{23}}}$
Total number of molecules ${\mathrm{=3 \times 10^{23}=3}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The weight of ${\mathrm{1 \times 10^{22}}}$ molecules of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ is ____(approx) .
explanation:
Weight of ${\mathrm{6.023 \times 10^{23}}}$ (Avogadro's number)$={\mathrm{M w}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=249 \mathrm{~g}}}$
$=1 \mathrm{~mol} \text { of } \mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Weight of ${\mathrm{1 \times 10^{22}}}$ molecules of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CuSO}_{4} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
$=\dfrac{249 \times 1 \times 10^{22}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}=4.14 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many times the vapour density of glucose is multiple of vapour density of formaldehyde?
subject: Chemistry

question: How many molecules of benzene $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)$ are there in 1 L of benzene? Specific gravity of benzene is 0.88 . Express your answer in terms of $10^{24}$ molecules to the nearest possible integer.
explanation:
Molar mass of benzene $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)=12 \times 6+1 \times 6=78 \mathrm{~g}$
1 litre of benzene $=1000 \mathrm{~mL}$
Mass of $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ of benzene $=$ volume $\times$ density $ = 1000 \times 0.88 = 880\,{\rm{g}}$
$\therefore 78\,{\rm{g}}\,$ of benzene $ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}{\rm{molecules}}$
$880\,{\rm{g}}$ of benzene $ = \frac{{6.023 \times {{10}^{23}} \times 880}}{{78}}$
$ = 6.795 \times {10^{24}}{\rm{molecules}}. \Rightarrow 7$
subject: Chemistry

question: Number of moles of electrons in $0.5\,\,{\rm{mol}}\,\,{{\rm{N}}^{3 - }}$ ions will be _____.
explanation: $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ ion has 10 electrons. Thus, $0.5 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ ions will have $5 \mathrm{~mol}$ electrons.
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour density of a mixture containing $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is 38.3 at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ in $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of the mixture(approx.)
explanation:
Mol. mass of mixture $=2 \times 38.3=76.6$
No. of moles in $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of mixture $=\dfrac{500}{76.6}$
Let a g of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is present in mixture.
Moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}+$ Moles of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}=$ Moles of mixture
$\dfrac{a}{46}+\dfrac{500-a}{76.6}=\dfrac{500}{76.6}$ or $a=100.52 \mathrm{~g}$
Moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ in mixture $=\dfrac{100.52}{46}=2.19$$ \approx 2\,$
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of sulphur atoms present in 0.1 mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{8}}}$ molecules is ____ ${\mathrm{\times 10^{23}}}$
explanation:
Total number of atoms in a given amount of substance ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \mathrm{n} \times}}$ Atomicity
Number of ${\mathrm{S}}$ atoms ${\mathrm{=6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.1 \times 8}}$$\approx 4.82 \times 10^{23}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If the cost of 360 g of glucose ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)}}$ is Rs 60, then the cost of glucose per mole is Rs ____ .
explanation:
Number of moles ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { Mass of a substance }}{\text { Molar mass of a substance }}}}$$=\dfrac{360 \mathrm{~g}}{180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=2 \mathrm{~mol}$
Now,
2 mol of glucose cost ${\mathrm{=}}$ Rs 60
$\therefore \quad {\rm{1}}\,{\rm{mol}}$ of glucose cost ${\rm{ = }}$ Rs 30
subject: Chemistry

question: If ${\mathrm{10^{21}}}$ molecules are removed from 140 mg of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}$, then the number of moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}$ left is ${\mathrm{3.34 \times 10^{\mathrm{X}}}}$ moles. The value of ' X ' is ____.
explanation:
140 mg of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}=140 \times 10^{-3}=0.14 \mathrm{~g}}}$
28 g of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}=6.022 \times 10^{23}}}$ molecules
${\mathrm{\therefore 0.14 \mathrm{~g}}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}=\dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{28} \times 0.14}}$$\begin{aligned}& =0.03011 \times 10^{23} \\& =3.011 \times 10^{21} \text { molecules }\end{aligned}$
Now, ${\mathrm{10^{21}}}$ molecules are removed,
So, remaining molecules ${\mathrm{=3.011 \times 10^{21}-10^{21}}}$$\begin{aligned}& =10^{21}(3.011-1) \\& =2.011 \times 10^{21} \text { molecules }\end{aligned}$
Now, ${\mathrm{6.022 \times 10^{23}}}$ molecules ${\mathrm{=1}}$ mole
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 2.011 \times 10^{21}}}$ molecules ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{2.011 \times 10^{21}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =0.334 \times 10^{-2} \\& =3.34 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{moles}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore $ Value of ' X ' is -3 .
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ in one litre is
explanation:
Density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$
Mass of water $=$ Density $\times$ Volume$\, = 1 \times 1000 = 1000\,{\rm{g}}$
$[\because 1\,{\text{L}} = 1000{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{cc]}}$
${\rm{No}}{\rm{.}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{moles}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}\, = \frac{{{\rm{ Mass }}\,{\rm{of }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}}{{{\rm{ Molar }}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{ of}}\,{\rm{ }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}}$
$ = \frac{{1000}}{{18}} = 55.55$
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of electrons in a mole of hydrogen molecule is :
explanation:
No. of electron
= No. of electrons per molecule ${\rm{ \times }}$ No. of molecules
${\rm{ = 2 \times 6}}{\rm{.023 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}{\rm{ = 12}}{\rm{.046 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour density of ozone is
explanation:
We know that,
Molecular weight of compound or molecules ${\rm{ = 2 \times V}}{\rm{.D}}$.
Vapour density (V. D.) of ozone molecules${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{M}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{. of }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{48}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 24}}{\rm{.}}$
${\rm{Hence,V}}{\rm{.D}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}\,\,{\rm{is}}\,{\rm{24}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The largest number of molecules is in
subject: Chemistry

question: Volume of a gas at NTP is $1.12 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$. The number of molecules in it is
explanation:
Given, ${\rm{V}} = 1.12 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^3}$
$\because $ $22400\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$of the gas at NTP has molecules$ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}$
$\therefore \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 1.12 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^3}$ of the gas at NTP will have molecules.
$ = \frac{{6.023 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{22400}} \times 1.12 \times {10^{ - 7}}$
$ = 3.01 \times {10^{12}}{\rm{ molecules }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If $1 \mathrm{~mL}$ of water contains 20 drops then number of molecules in one drop of water is
explanation:
$\because $ $22400\,{\rm{mL}}$ water contains water molecules
=$6.023 \times {10^{23}}$ molecules
$\therefore {\rm{In}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{mL,the}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{number}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{water}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{molecules}}$
$ = \frac{{6.023 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{22400}}{\rm{molecules}}$
Since, $1\,{\rm{mL}}$ contains 20 drops.
Therefore, number of water molecules in 1 drop$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{22400 \times 20} \\& =1.344 \times 10^{18} \text { molecules }\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of sodium atoms in 2 moles of sodium ferrocyanide is
explanation:
The formula of sodium ferrocyanide is ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_4}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{({\rm{CN}})}_6}} \right]$ suggests that it has four sodium atoms.
Number of ${\rm{Na}}$-atoms
=number of moles $ \times $number of atoms per molecule$ \times $Avagadro's number
$ = 2 \times 4 \times 6.023 \times {10^{23}}\,{\rm{Na - atoms}}$
${\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{48}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}\,{\rm{Na - atoms}}$
$ \sim 48 \times {10^{23}}{\text{Na - atoms}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For $14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CO}$, the wrong statement is
explanation:
(1) 28 g of CO occupies
$ = 22.4\,\,{\rm{L}}$\,of CO at NTP
$\therefore $ 14 g of CO occupies
$ = \frac{{22.4}}{{28}} \times 14\,{\rm{L}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{CO}}\,{\rm{at}}\,{\rm{NTP}}$
$ = 11.2\,{\rm{L}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{CO}}\,{\rm{at}}\,{\rm{NTP}}$
Thus 14 g of CO occupies 11.2L volume at NTP
(2) Number of moles of CO
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{CO}}}}{{{\rm{molar}}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{CO}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{14}}\,{\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{28}}\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}$ mole of CO
Thus 14 g CO equal to $\frac{1}{2}$ mole of CO.
(3)As the molar mass of CO and ${{\rm{N}}_2}$ gas are the same ,thus 14g of CO corresponds to same moles of ${{\text{N}}_2}$ gas.
(4) 28 g of CO contains
$ = 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{\rm{molecules}}$
$\therefore $ 14 g of CO contains
$ = \frac{{6.022 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{28}} \times 14$
$ = 3.011 \times {10^{23}}{\rm{molecules}}$
Thus,14 g of CO corresponds to $3.011 \times {10^{23}}{\rm{molecules}}$.
Hence, option (1) is wrong, while rest of the options are correct.

options.text: it corresponds to same mole of $\mathrm{CO}$ and nitrogen gas

subject: Chemistry

question: The weight of $112 \mathrm{~mL}$ of oxygen at NTP is
explanation:
At NTP/STP
${\text{n}} = \frac{{\text{V}}}{{22400(\,{\text{1mL}})}} = \frac{{\text{w}}}{{\text{M}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{w}} = \frac{{{\rm{V}} \times {\rm{M}}}}{{22,400}}$
Given,${\rm{V}} = 112\,{\rm{mL}}$ and molar mass of oxygen$ = 32\,{\rm{g}}/{\rm{mol}}$
$\therefore \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{w}} = \frac{{112 \times 32}}{{22400\,{\rm{mL}}}} \Rightarrow {\rm{w}} = 0.16\,{\rm{g}}$
Hence, weight of $112\,{\rm{mL}}\,$of oxygen at${\rm{NTP}}/{\rm{STP}} = 0.16\,{\rm{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A gas is found to have a formula $(\mathrm{CO})_{\mathrm{x}}$. If its vapour density is 70 , the value of x is
explanation:
Molecular weight$ = 2 \times {\text{vapour}}$
density $ = 2 \times 70 = 140\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\because $$$${({\rm{CO}})_{\rm{x}}} = 140$
$\therefore {(12 + 16)_{\rm{x}}} = 140$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {(28)_{\rm{x}}} = {(28)_5}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{x}} = 5$
subject: Chemistry

question: One atom of an element weighs $1.8 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}$. Its atomic mass is
explanation:
Mass of 1 atom of an element
$ = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 22}}\,{\rm{g}}$
Mass of $6.023 \times {10^{23}}$ atoms of an element
$ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}} \times 1.8 \times {10^{ - 22}}\,{\rm{g}}$
$ = 6.023 \times 1.8 \times 10\,{\rm{g}} = 108.36\,{\rm{g}}$
Hence, atomic mass of element$ = 108.36\,{\rm{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour density of a gas is 11.2 . The volume occupied by one gram of the gas at STP is
explanation:
Here, vapour density of gas $ = 11.2$

=1L

question: The volume of a gas at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 760 mm pressure is 22.4 cc . The no. of molecules present in this volume is

Given are NTP. conditions
So, At NTP condition no.of moles.
${\rm{n}} = \frac{{{\rm{ Vol}}{\rm{. of gas at NTP }}}}{{22.4\,{\rm{L}}}}$
${\rm{n}} = \frac{{22.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{22.4}}$
$ \Rightarrow {10^{ - 3}}{\rm{moles}}$
No. of molecules $=$ no. of moles $\times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$\therefore$ No.of molecules $=10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}$

question: Assuming the human body to be $80 \%$ of water, the number of molecules of water present in the body of a person, of mass 65 kg , are
explanation:
100 kg of human body contains 80 kg of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
65 kg of human body contains
$=\dfrac{80}{100} \times 65=52 \mathrm{~kg}$ water
18 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$52 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ contains?
$=\dfrac{52 \times 10^{3} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{18}$
$=17.39 \times 10^{26}$ molecules

question: In which of the following pairs do 1 g of each have an equal number of molecules?
explanation: $\lg$ of those pairs have equal number of molecules which have equal molecular weight.$\mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{N}_{2}$ both have same molar mass.

question: Same mass of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ and acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ contain
explanation:

1 mole of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ has 6 mole $\mathrm{C}, 6$ mole O , and 12 mole H atoms
Similarly, 3 moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ has 6 mole C , 6 mole O and 12 mole H atoms.

question: The number of molecules and moles in 2.8375 litres of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}}$ at STP are respectively
explanation:

${\mathrm{=0.126 \mathrm{~mol} \simeq 0.125 \mathrm{~mol}}}$

(or) ${\mathrm{7.527 \times 10^{22}}}$

question:
Assertion: One mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$ contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\mathrm{HCl}$.


Reason: $36.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ also contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\mathrm{HCl}$
explanation:
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{HCl}=36.5 \mathrm{~g} /$ mole
According to the mole concept, one mole of any substance contains Avogadro's number
$\left(6.023 \times 10^{23}\right)$ of molecules. So, in the case of $\mathrm{HCl}, 36.5$ grams of $\mathrm{HCl}$ also contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\mathrm{HCl}$. So, the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Atomicity of oxygen is 2 .


Reason: 1 mole of an element contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms.
explanation:
The number of atoms present in a molecule of a gaseous element is called its atomicity. For example, $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ has two atoms, and hence its atomicity is 2 .
1 mole of an element contains Avagadro number of atoms. So, the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, and $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ contain the same number of moles under identical conditions.


Reason: Under identical conditions, $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ,gas and $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ gas contain the same number of oxygen atoms.
explanation:
Under identical conditions, $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ gas and $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ gas contain the same number of moles and hence, the same number of molecules but not the same number of atoms.
1 mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ contains $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ molecules and $2 \mathrm{~N}$ oxygen atoms.
1 mole of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ contains $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ molecules and $3 \mathrm{~N}$ oxygen atoms So, the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

Reason: Both contain $1 \mathrm{~g}$ atom of carbon which contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ carbon atoms.
explanation:
$44 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \equiv 1$ mole $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \equiv 1 \mathrm{~g}$-atom of " $\mathrm{C}$ "
$16 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_{4} \equiv 1$ mole $\mathrm{CH}_{4} \equiv 1 \mathrm{~g}$-atom of " $\mathrm{C}$ "
$12 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{C}(1$ mole $)$ contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ carbon atoms. So, the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Molar volume of gases change considerably with temperature and pressure.


Reason: Molar volume of a substance is the volume occupied by 1 mole of that substance.
explanation: The volume of a gas is dependen on its temperature and pressure. Temperature effects the kinetic energy. Pressure change effects the intermoelcular attraction, which inturn allows for expansion or contraction. So, the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: 3 moles of azide ion and 1 mole of nitride ion contain same amount of charge.


Reason: 1 mole of azide ion and 1 mole of nitride ion have equal number of nitrogen atoms.
explanation:
Azide ion is $\mathrm{N}_{3}^{-}$
$\text { charge in } 1 \text { mole }=1 \mathrm{~F}$
$\therefore$ charge in 3 mole $=3 \mathrm{~F}$
Nitride ion is $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$
charge in 1 mole $=3 \mathrm{~F}$
One mole of azide ion has $3 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ nitrogen atoms but one mole of nitride ion has only $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ atoms. So, the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
The following substances are present in different containers
(i) one gram atom of nitrogen
(ii) one mole of calcium
(iii) One atom of silver
(iv) One mole of oxygen molecules
(v) $\mathrm{10^{23}}$ atoms of carbon
(vi) One gram of iron
The correct order of increasing masses (in grams) is/are
explanation:
(i) $\mathrm{14 \mathrm{~g}}$ Nitrogen (ii) $\mathrm{40 \mathrm{~g}}$ Calcium
(iii) $\mathrm{\dfrac{108}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}=1.79 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}}$ Silver
(iv) $\mathrm{32 \mathrm{~g}}$ Oxygen (v) $\mathrm{1.99 \mathrm{~g}}$ Carbon (vi) 1 $\mathrm{\mathrm{g}}$ Iron
Hence the correct order of increasing masses is (iii) $\mathrm{ < }$ (vi) $\mathrm{ < }$ (v) $\mathrm{ < }$ (i) $\mathrm{ < }$ (iv) $\mathrm{ < }$ (ii)
subject: Chemistry

question:

explanation:

question: The number of atoms present in 4.25 grams of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is approximately.
explanation:
Molar mass of ammonia $\mathrm{=17 \mathrm{~g} /}$ mole
No. of moles $\mathrm{=(4.25 / 17)=0.25}$ moles
No. of atoms $\mathrm{=4 \times 0.25 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
$\rm{=6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A compound has $\mathrm{20 \%}$ of nitrogen by weight. If one molecule of the compound contains two nitrogen atoms, the molecular weight of the compound is
explanation:
Mass of two nitrogen atoms $\mathrm{=28 \mathrm{~g}}$
Compound has $\mathrm{20 \%}$ nitrogen, means
$\mathrm{20 \mathrm{~g}}$ nitrogen is present in $\mathrm{100 \mathrm{~g}}$ compound
$\mathrm{28 \mathrm{~g}}$ nitrogen is present in ? g compound ?
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{28 \times 100}{20}=140 \mathrm{~g}}$ compound
subject: Chemistry

question: Total number of atoms present in $\mathrm{64 \mathrm{gm}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{2} \rightarrow}$ Contains 3 atoms per molecule
no. of moles, $\mathrm{n=\dfrac{\text { wt. }}{\text { M.wt. }}=\dfrac{64}{64}=1 \mathrm{~mole}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ No.of molecules $\mathrm{=6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
and No.of atoms $\mathrm{=3 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{4.0 \mathrm{~g}}$ of caustic soda $\mathrm{(\mathrm{NaOH})(\operatorname{mol}}$ mass 40$\mathrm{)}$ contains same number of sodium ions as are present in
explanation:
$\mathrm{\dfrac{4}{40} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{10}}$ atoms of sodium each $\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ molecule contains $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}}$ions
In $\mathrm{0.5 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ of $\mathrm{100 \mathrm{ml}}$, no. of sodium ions
$\rm{=\dfrac{\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{ml})}{1000} \times 2 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.5 \times 100 \times 2 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{1000}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{10}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If $\mathrm{224 \mathrm{ml}}$. of a triatomic gas has a mass of $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ at $\mathrm{273 \mathrm{~K}}$ and $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~atm}}$ pressure, then the mass of one atom is
explanation:
No. of moles of triatomic gas
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{224}}\,\,{\text{ml}}}}{{{\text{22400}}\,\,{\text{ml}}\,{\text{mol}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}}}{\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}{\text{mol}}$
No. of molecules
${\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}{\text{ mol}} \times \left( {{\text{6}}{\text{.02}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{23}}}}{\text{molecules}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}} \right)$
$\mathrm{=6.02 \times 10^{21}}$
no. of atoms of gas
$\mathrm{=3 \times 6.02 \times 10^{21}=18.06 \times 10^{21}=1.806 \times 10^{22}}$
$\mathrm{1.806 \times 10^{22}}$ atoms has mass $\mathrm{=1 \mathrm{~g}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 1}$ atom mass $\mathrm{=\dfrac{1}{1.806 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~g}}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}}{1.806}}$
$\mathrm{=0.554 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}}$
$\mathrm{=5.54 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{\mathrm{xL}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}$ at S.T.P. contains $\mathrm{3 \times 10^{22}}$ molecules. The number of molecules in $\mathrm{\mathrm{x} / 2 \mathrm{~L}}$ of ozone at S.T.P. will be
explanation: Equal volumes of all gases under similar conditions of temperature and pressure will have equal number of molecules.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following will not have a mass of $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~g}}$ ?
explanation:
0.1 mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=0.1 \times 100=10 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}=\dfrac{1.51 \times 10^{23}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \times 40=10 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ca}^{+2}}$ ions
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}=0.16 \times 60=9.6 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{-2}}$ ions
${\text{Br = }}\frac{{{\text{7}}{\text{.525}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{22}}}}}}{{{\text{6}}{\text{.2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{23}}}}}} \times {\text{80 = 10}}\,{\text{g Br atoms}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
If $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ ionises as
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}$. Then total number of ions produced by 0.1 mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ will be
explanation:
1 mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ produces 3 mole ions
$\mathrm{\therefore 0.1}$ mole $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ produces 0.3 mole ions
Number of ions $\mathrm{=0.3 \mathrm{~mole} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}}$
$\mathrm{=1.8 \times 10^{23}}$ ions.
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of oxygen atoms present in $\mathrm{14.6 \mathrm{~g}}$ of magnesium bicarbonate is:
explanation:
Magnesium bicarbonate, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}}$, molar mass is $\mathrm{146 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mole}}$.
$\mathrm{146 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}}$ contains 6 mole oxygen atoms.
$\mathrm{14.6 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{HCO}_{3}\right)_{2}}$ contains ?
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{14.6 \times 6}{146}=0.6 \mathrm{~mole}=0.6 \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{A}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 10 grams of each $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~N}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ are kept in three bottles. The correct order of arrangement of bottles containing decreasing number of molecules is
explanation:
1 mole means $\mathrm{6.02 \times 10^{23}}$ molecules
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ No. of molecules $\mathrm{\propto}$ no. of moles
no. of moles $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { molar mass }}}$
Here no. of moles $\mathrm{=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}>\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}>\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}}$ (as the given masses are same)
subject: Chemistry

question: Two elements $\rm{{\text{X}}}$ and $\rm{{\text{Y}}}$ combine in gaseous state to form $\rm{{\text{XY}}}$ in the ratio 1:35.5 by mass. The mass of $\rm{{\text{Y}}}$ that will be required to react with $\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{X}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{X}$ combines with $\mathrm{35.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ ' $\mathrm{y}$ '
$\mathrm{\therefore 2 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{X}}$ combines with $\mathrm{\dfrac{2 \times 35.5}{1}=71 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Y}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many gram of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$ are present in $\mathrm{0.25 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of it?
explanation:
$\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=18 \mathrm{~g}}$ (molar mass)
$\mathrm{\therefore 0.25 \mathrm{~mol}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\dfrac{18 \times 0.25}{1}=4.5 \mathrm{~g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many molecules are present in $\mathrm{22400 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}$ of a gas at STP?
explanation: 1 mole of gas at STP occupies $\mathrm{22400 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}$ volume and contains $\mathrm{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$ molecules
subject: Chemistry

question: How many molecules of ammonia gas are present in $\mathrm{67.2 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$, measured at S.T.P.?
explanation:
No.of moles $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { Volume }\left(\mathrm{dm}^{3}\right) \text { at STP }}{22.4}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{67.2}{22.4}=3 \text { moles }}$
No. of molecules $\mathrm{=}$ no. of moles $\mathrm{\times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}$
$\mathrm{=3 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}=1.8 \times 10^{24}}$ molecules
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the mass of $\mathrm{33.6 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ of methane gas at S.T.P.?
explanation:
No.of moles $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { Volume }\left(\mathrm{dm}^{3}\right) \text { at STP }}{22.4}}$ $\mathrm{=\dfrac{33.6}{22.4}=1.5 \mathrm{moles}}$
Mass $\mathrm{(}$ in gram $\mathrm{)=}$ No. of moles $\mathrm{\times}$ gram molar mass
$\mathrm{=1.5 \times 16=24 \mathrm{~g}}$
$\mathrm{=2.4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
How many moles of urea are present in $\mathrm{5.4 \mathrm{~g}}$ ?
$\left( {{\text{Molar}}\,{\text{mass = 60 g mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}} \right)$
explanation:
No.of moles $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { Mass in gram }}{\text { gram molar mass }}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{5.4}{60}=0.09}$ moles
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the volume occupied by $\mathrm{16 \mathrm{~g}}$ methane gas at STP?
explanation:
1 mole any gas at STP occupies $\mathrm{22.4 \mathrm{~L}}$ (or) $\mathrm{22400 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}$
Methane molar mass $\mathrm{=16 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mole}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CH}_{4}}$ occupies $\mathrm{22400 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the volume (in $\mathrm{\mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ ) occupied by $\mathrm{75 \mathrm{~g}}$ ethane at S.T.P?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\underset{\text { Ethane }}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}} \rightarrow}$ Molar mass $\mathrm{=30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
1 mole any gas at STP occupies $\mathrm{22.4 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g}}$ of ethane $\mathrm{=22.4 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ at STP
$\mathrm{\therefore 75 \mathrm{~g}}$ of ethane $\mathrm{=\dfrac{22.4 \times 75}{30}=56.0 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{10^{21}}$ molecules are removed from $\mathrm{200 \mathrm{mg}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$. The moles of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ left are
explanation:
1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ molecule
$\mathrm{=6.023 \times 10^{23}}$ molecules of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{=44 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 10^{21}}$ molecules of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{44}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \times 10^{21} \mathrm{gCO}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{=7.31 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~g}=73.1 \mathrm{mg}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ left $\mathrm{=200-73.1=126.9 \mathrm{mg}}$
$\mathrm{=126.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g}}$
Hence, moles of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ left $\mathrm{=\dfrac{\text { given mass }}{\text { molar mass }}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{126.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g}}{44 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}}$
$\mathrm{=2.88 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If we consider that$\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{6}}}$, in place of $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{12}}}}$,  mass of carbon atom is taken to be the relative mass unit, the mass of one mole of a substance will
explanation:
Relative atomic mass
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{element}}}}{{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1}}{{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{th}}}}}}{\rm{part}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Carbon - 12}}}}$
${\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{element}}}}{{{\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C - 12}}}}{\rm{ \times 12}}$
Now if we use $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{6}}}$ in place $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{12}}}}$ the formula becomes Relative atomic mass
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{element}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{one}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atom}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C - 12}}}}{\rm{ \times 6}}$
$\therefore $ Relative atomic mass decrease twice

options.text: Be a function of the molecular mass of the substance

subject: Chemistry

question: Four one–litre flasks are separately filled with the gases hydrogen, helium, oxygen and ozone at the same room temperature and pressure. The ratio of total number of atoms of these gases present in the different flasks would be
explanation:
Equal volumes of gases contain equal no. of molecules at same room temperature and pressure.

question: How many valence electrons are present in 0.53 g of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ ?
explanation:
In ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$, sodium exists as ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ and carbonate exists as ${\rm{CO}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}$. In ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$, number of valence electrons are 8. In ${\rm{CO}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}$, number of valence electrons are 24.
Total number of valence electrons in ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 2}}\left( {\rm{8}} \right){\rm{ + 24 = 40}}$
Hence, 106 g of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ contains ${\rm{40 \times 6}}{\rm{.023 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}$ valence electrons.
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{0}}{\rm{.53}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{contains}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.53 \times }}\frac{{{\rm{40}}}}{{{\rm{106}}}}{\rm{ \times 6}}{\rm{.023 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2 \times 6}}{\rm{.023 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}$ valence electrons.
Note : Number of valence electrons in Na is 1 but in ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ is 8.
subject: Chemistry

${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.06}}$ moles.

${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.36 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$
Total no. of moles ${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.07 + 0}}{\rm{.06 = 0}}{\rm{.13}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$
Total number of molecules ${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.07 \times 06}}{\rm{.023 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{23}}}}$

explanation:

question: The ratio masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecule is
explanation:
The number of moles is given by
${\rm{n = }}\frac{{{\rm{weight}}\,\,{\rm{(W)}}}}{{{\rm{molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{weight}}\,\,{\rm{(M)}}}}$
Thus, ratio of moles of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is given by
$\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}\left( {\frac{{\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}}}{{\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}{{{{\rm{W}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}} \right){\rm{ \times }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{28}}}}{{{\rm{32}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{7}}}{{{\rm{32}}}}$
Hence, ratio of ${{\rm{n}}_{{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{n}}_{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{is}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{7:32}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Number of atoms in the following samples of substances is the largest in:
explanation:

= 1 moleof chlorine
$ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}\,\,atoms$
(3) 127 gm of iodine =1 mole of I atoms $ = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}\,\,atoms$
(4) 48.0 gm of magnesium $ = \frac{{48.0}}{{24.0}}$
2 moles of Mg $ = 2 \times 6.023 \times {10^{23}}\,\,atoms$
$\therefore $ 4.0 gm ${H_2}$ has the largest number of atoms.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Atoms are the smallest unit of matter that can take part in a chemical reaction.
Molecules are a group of atoms that can exist independently and retain all properties of a substance.
explanation: Atoms are indeed the smallest unit of matter that participate in chemical reactions, and molecules, which are made up of atoms, can exist independently while retaining the properties of the substance they form. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:

All mixtures are homogeneous and have uniform composition throughout.
The components of a mixture do not necessarily have to be in a fixed ratio.
explanation: Mixtures can be homogeneous or heterogeneous; not all mixtures have a uniform composition. The components of a mixture are not in a fixed ratio which is a characteristic of mixtures. Correct option is (3)

question:

Pure substances have fixed composition and can be elements or compounds.
Compounds are pure substances that can be separated into simpler substances by physical methods.
explanation: Pure substances do have a fixed composition and can be either elements or compounds. However, compounds cannot be separated into their component elements by physical methods, but can be separated  only by chemical methods. Correct option is (2)

explanation: The state of matter is indeed changed by altering temperature and pressure; however, transitions from solid to liquid and then to gas typically involve increase in temperatures. Correct option is (2)

explanation: Solids are generally incompressible due to the tight packing of particles, which also have a very limited degree of freedom of movement. Correct option is (3)

Gases completely occupy the container in which they are placed.

In liquids, particles are tightly packed in an orderly fashion.
Liquids do not have a definite shape but have a definite volume.
explanation: In liquids, particles are not packed in an orderly fashion; instead, they have some freedom to move around, which is why they do not have a definite shape but do have a definite volume. Correct option is (3)

explanation: Gases do not have a definite shape or volume because their particles are much farther apart compared to solids and liquids, allowing for rapid and free movement. Correct option is (1)

question:
