(At. mass: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}=39 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{Cr}=52 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{u}}}$ )
explanation:
Molar mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}=2 \times(39)}}$$+2 \times(52)+7 \times(16)$
${\mathrm{78+104+112=294 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$
Percentage of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}=\dfrac{104}{294} \times 100=35.37 \%}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Percentage of iron in ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]$ is
explanation:
${\rm{\% }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Iron}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( {{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Fe}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ \times 100}}} \right)}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( {{\rm{56 \times 100}}} \right)}}{{{\rm{329}}}}{\rm{ = 17}}{\rm{.02\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The percentage weight of Zn in white vitriol ${\rm{[ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O]}}$ is approximately equal to (Atomic mass of Zn = 65, S = 32, O = 16 and H = 1)
explanation:
Molecular weight of ${\rm{ZnS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O = 65 + 32 + (4 \times 16) + 7(2 \times 1 + 16) = 287}}$
$\therefore $ Percentage mass of zinc (Zn)
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{65}}}}{{{\rm{287}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 22}}{\rm{.65\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 400 mg of a capsule contains 100 mg of ferrous fumarate. The percentage of iron present in the capsule is approximately
explanation:
The molecular formula of ferrous fumarate is ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Fe}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ (Molecular weight = 170 g/mol)
One molecule of ferrous fumarate contains one iron atom
170 g of ferrous fumarate contains 56 g of iron
100 mg of ferrous fumarate contains
$\frac{{{\rm{56 \times 100}}}}{{{\rm{170}}}}{\rm{ = 32}}{\rm{.9}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mg}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{iron}}$
% iron in the capsule
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{iron}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{capsule}}}}{{{\rm{400}}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{32}}{\rm{.9}}}}{{{\rm{400}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 8}}{\rm{.2\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A gas mixture contains 25% He and 75% ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ by volume at a given temperature and pressure. The percentage by mass of methane in the mixture is approximately
explanation:
${\rm{75}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mL}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + 25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mL}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{He = 100ml}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{gas}}$.
16 g of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ has volume 22400 mL (At STP)
Mass of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ has volume 22400 mL (At STP)
Mass of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ by volume ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{75 \times 16}}}}{{{\rm{22400}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.05357}}$
Mass of He by volume ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{25 \times 4}}}}{{{\rm{22400}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.004464}}$
Mass percentage of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.05357}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.05357 + 0}}{\rm{.004464}}}}{\rm{ \times 100}} \approx {\rm{92\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is the percentage of carbon in urea?
${\rm{(Atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C = 12,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{H = 1,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N = 14,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{O = 16)}}$
explanation:
Molar mass of urea ${\rm{(i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{CON}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{)}}$m
$\begin{array}{l}{\rm{ = 2 \times atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N + 4 \times atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} \\ {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{H + 1 \times atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{O + 1 \times atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}\end{array}$
${\rm{ = 2 \times 14 + 4 \times 1 + 1 \times 16 + 1 \times 12 = 60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
As we know that,
Percentage or mass % of an element
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{that}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{element}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{compound}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{compound}}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Percentage}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C = }}\frac{{{\rm{1 \times 12}}}}{{{\rm{60}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 20\% }}$

question: What is the SI unit of density ?
explanation:
Density of substance is defined as its amount of mass per unit volume. So, SI unit of density can be obtained by using the following relation.
${\rm{SI}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{unit}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{density = }}\frac{{{\rm{SI}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{unit}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{SI}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{unit}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{volume}}}}$
$\frac{{{\rm{kg}}}}{{{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$

question: The temperature of ${\rm{3}}{{\rm{2}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$ is equivalent to
explanation:
The temperature of °F and °C are related to each other by the following relationship:
${\rm{^\circ F = }}\frac{{\rm{9}}}{{\rm{5}}}\left( {{\rm{^\circ C}}} \right){\rm{ + 32}}$
$^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{F = }}\frac{{{\rm{9 \times 32}}}}{{\rm{5}}}{\rm{ + 32 = 57}}{\rm{.6 + 32}}$
$^{\rm{^\circ }}{\rm{F = 89}}{\rm{.6}}$
The temperature of ${\rm{3}}{{\rm{2}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$ is equivalent to ${\rm{89}}{\rm{.}}{{\rm{6}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{F}}$.

question: 12 g of an element reacts with 32 g of oxygen. What is the equivalent weight of the element?
explanation:
Equivalent weight of an element will be its weight that reacts with 8 g of oxygen. Given that 12 g of the element reacts with 32 g of oxygen, then the mass of that element reacts with 8 g of oxygen : $\dfrac{12 \times 8}{32}=3 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight of the element is 3 g

question:
Match the following
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951760/preptracker/questions/q_103545_question_1764951759676.png
\[{\text{(M}} = \] Molecular Weight)
The correct answer is
explanation:
Equivalent Weight $=\dfrac{\text { Molecular weight }(\mathrm{M})}{\mathrm{n} \text { factor }}$
A - R; $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{-2}$
$\mathrm{n}-$ factor $=$ charge on the cation or anion $=2$.
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight $=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{2}$
B - P; In acidic medium, ' Mn ' in $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ change its oxidation number from +7 to +2 .
Hence, n -factor $=5$.
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight $=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{5}$
C - S; In acidic medium, ' $\mathrm{Cr}^{\prime}$ in $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ change its oxidation number from +6 to +3 . Hence, n -factor $=6$.
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight $=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{6}$
D - Q; In neutral medium, ' $\mathrm{Mn}^{\prime}$ in $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ change its oxidation number from +7 to +4 .
Hence, n -factor $=3$.
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight $=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}}{3}$

question: 0.43 g of a metal of valence 2 was dissolved in 50 mL of   $0.5{\text{M}}$  ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$solution. The unreacted acid required 14.2 mL of 1 M NaOH solution for neutralization. The atomic weight of the metal is
explanation:
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ added
$=\mathrm{MV}_{(\mathrm{L})}=0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}=0.025 \mathrm{~mol}$
Moles of NaOH reacted with
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\mathrm{MV}_{(\mathrm{L})}=1 \times 14.2 \times 10^{-3}=0.71 \mathrm{~mol}$
Since 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ reacts with 2 moles of NaOH , Moles of unreacted
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\dfrac{0.0142}{2}=0.0071 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\therefore$ Moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ that reacted with metal
$=0.025-0.0071=0.017 \mathrm{~mol}$.
The metal of valency 2 reacts as:
$\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{MSO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
So, 1 mole of metal reacts with 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
$\therefore$ Moles of metal $=0.0179 \mathrm{~mol}$.
$\therefore$ Atomic weight $=\dfrac{\text { Weight }}{\text { No. of moles }}$
$=\dfrac{0.43}{0.0179}=24.02 \mathrm{u} \approx 24 \mathrm{u}$

question: If 2g of NaOH is dissolved to make 200 mL solution at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molarity $(\mathrm{M})$ at $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
explanation:
Molarity $=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{\mathrm{M}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~mL})}=\dfrac{2}{40} \times \dfrac{1000}{200}$
$=0.25$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
But as temperature increases, water expands, the volume of solution increases. Hence at $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molarity will be less than 0.25 .
subject: Chemistry

question: If the density of a 2M solution of ethylene glycol in water is $1.11 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$, the molality (in ' m ') of the solution is approximately
explanation:
The relation between molarity (M) and molality ( m ) is
$\mathrm{m}=\dfrac{1000 \times \mathrm{M}}{(1000 \times \mathrm{d})-(\mathrm{M} \times \text { molar mass })}$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}=\dfrac{1000 \times 2}{(1000 \times 1.11)-(2 \times 62.07)}$
$=\dfrac{2000}{1110-124.14}=2.028 \cong 2.03 \mathrm{~m}$
Hence, molality of the solution is 2.03 m .
subject: Chemistry

question: 100 g of a mixture of NaOH and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is neutralised by 100 mL of   $0.5{\text{M}}\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}.$   What is the amount of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ present in the mixture?
explanation:
Only NaOH is neutralised by $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
Mass of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=\dfrac{\mathrm{M} \times \text { molar mass } \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{mL})}{1000}$
$=\dfrac{0.5 \times 98 \times 100}{1000}=4.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
$2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$2\,{\text{mole}}\quad \,\,\,\,1\,{\text{mole}}$
$2 \times 40\,{\text{g}}\quad \,\,98\,{\text{g}}$
$?\quad \quad \quad \quad 4.9\,{\text{g}}$
$=\dfrac{4.9 \times 2 \times 40}{98}=4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NaOH}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}=100-4=96 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 25 mL of 0.1 N NaOH solution neutralises 12.5 mL of HCl solution. The amount of water needed to convert 500 mL of such HCl solution to 0.1 N is
explanation:
$\underset{\text { (base) }}{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}=\underset{\text { (acid) }}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}$ (neutralisation)
$25 \times 0.1=12.5 \times \mathrm{N}_{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{N}_{2}=0.2 \mathrm{~N}$
Dilution law, $\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}$
$0.2 \times 500=0.1 \times \mathrm{V}_{2}$
$\mathrm{V}_{2}=1000 \mathrm{~mL}$
$\therefore$ volume of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ to be added $=\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}$
$=1000-500=500 \mathrm{~mL}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is the equivalent weight of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ in acidic medium?
(Molecular weight of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}=158 \mathrm{~g}$ )
explanation:
In acidic medium, $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$, i.e.
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$changes to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ as follows:
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Hence, equivalent weight $=\dfrac{158}{5}=31.6 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, option (3) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: The weight of potassium dichromate (molecular weight $=294)$ required to prepare 0.04 N of 250 mL solution is
explanation:
Equivalent mass of $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ in acidic medium $=\dfrac{294}{6}=49 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{\operatorname{Mass}(\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{g} \cdot \mathrm{eq} \cdot \mathrm{mass}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~mL})}$
$\therefore$ Mass $=\dfrac{\mathrm{N} \times \text { eq. mass } \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{mL})}{1000}$
$=\dfrac{0.04 \times 49 \times 250}{1000}=0.49 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The approximate molarity of a solution in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$ that contains 13.50 g of NaCl dissolved in 452 mL of water is
explanation:
${\text{M}} = \frac{{{\text{ Mass }}}}{{{\text{ Molar mass }}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{{\text{V}}(\,{\text{mL}})}}$
$=\dfrac{13.50}{58.5} \times \dfrac{1000}{452}=0.51 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The molality (in  ${\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,)$ of 1 mole of solute in 50g of solvent is
explanation:
Given, moles of solute $\left(\mathrm{n}_{2}\right)=1 \mathrm{~mol}$, mass of solvent $\left(\mathrm{w}_{1}\right)=50 \mathrm{~g}$
$\because$ Molality $(\mathrm{m})=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{w}_{1}(\text { in g })}$
$\because \mathrm{m}=\dfrac{1000}{50}=20 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: While combusting in air,  4g of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas was completely converted into water. If $36 \mu$ mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ from air is dissolved into that water, what is the concentration of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ?
explanation:
The equation takes place as follows:
$\underset{4 \mathrm{~g}}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2}}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{36 \mathrm{~g}}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
$\because 36$ micromole ( $36 \times 10^{-6}$ mole) of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is dissolved in 36 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
$\therefore$ Conc. of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}=$ no. of moles $\times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~mL})}$
(density of water is $1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ )
$=\dfrac{36 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000}{36}=10^{-3} \mathrm{M}=1 \mathrm{mM}$
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: Calculate the molality of 1L solution of $93 \%$ $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ by $\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{V}\left[\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}=1.84 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}\right]$
explanation:
$\mathrm{d}=1.84 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $1000 \mathrm{~mL}=1000 \times 1.84=1840 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of solute in $1000 \mathrm{~mL}=930 \mathrm{~g}$ (given)
$\therefore$ Mass of solvent $=1840-930=910 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{m}=\dfrac{\text { Mass of solute }(\mathrm{g})}{\text { Molar mass of solute }} \times \dfrac{1000}{\text { Mass of solvent }(\mathrm{g})}$
$\mathrm{m}=\dfrac{930}{98} \times \dfrac{1000}{910}=10.42 \mathrm{~m}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following methods of expressing concentration are unitless?
explanation:
Mole fraction $ = \frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{solute}}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{solute}}}} + {{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{solvent }}}}}}$
Since both the numerator and denominator have the same units (mole), they cancel each other out, resulting in a unitless ratio.
Mass percent $(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})=\dfrac{\text { Mass of solute }(\mathrm{g})}{\text { Mass of solution }(\mathrm{g})} \times 100$
Since both the numerator and denominator have the same units (g), they cancel each other out, resulting in a unitless ratio.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous questions on reactions in solutions

question: If a substance ' A ' dissolves in solution of a mixture of ' B ' and ' C ' with their respective number of moles as $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}, \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}$ and $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}}$. Mole fraction of C in the solution is
explanation:
Mole fraction
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{No}}{\rm{.}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{moles}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{one}}\,{\rm{component}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}\,{\rm{no}}{\rm{.}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{moles}}}}$
So, mole fraction of ${\rm{C = }}\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{C}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{C}}}}}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry

question: The quantity which changes with temperature is
explanation: Molarity depends on volume of solution which is temperature dependent. Remaining concentration terms do not depend on volume, so they are temperature independent. So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question: To prepare 100 g of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{a}\,\,92 \%}}$ by weight solution of NaOH , how many g of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ is needed?
explanation:
Weight of ${\rm{NaOH}}$
${{\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{92}}\,{\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{100}}\,{\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}}}} \right){\rm{ \times (100}}\,{\rm{g}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution)}}}$
$ = 92\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{NaOH}}$
${{\rm{Weight}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{ = 100}}{\mkern 1mu} - {\rm{92}}{\mkern 1mu} }$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ = 8}}.{\rm{0}}\,{\rm{g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many mL of a solution of concentration ${\mathrm{100 \, \mathrm{mg} \, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}}}$ per mL is needed to prepare 10 mL of a solution of concentration ${\mathrm{20 \,\,\mathrm{mg}\, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}}}$ per mL .
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour density of a chloride of an element is 39.5 . The ${\mathrm{E w}}$ of the elements is 3.82 . The atomic weight of the element is ____  .
explanation:
${\mathrm{M w}}$ of metal chloride ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{MCl}_{x}\right)=M+x \times 35.5}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =2 \times \mathrm{VD}=2 \times 39.5 \\& =79.0\end{aligned}$
${\mathrm{M w=E w \times x}}$
Valency of metal ${\mathrm{=x}}$
Atomic weight of element ${\mathrm{=E w \times x}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{MCl}_{x}=3.82 \times x+x \times 35.5=79}}$
${\mathrm{x=\dfrac{79}{3.82+35.5} \approx 2}}$
Atomic weight ${\mathrm{=E w \times x=3.82 \times 2=7.64}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The molarity of 30 mass $\% \,\,{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4}$ solution of density $1.14 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ is ____ .
explanation:
Volume of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ solution, ${\rm{V}} = \frac{{\rm{m}}}{{\rm{d}}}$
$=\dfrac{100 \mathrm{~g}}{1.14 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}}=87.72 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
Amount of sulphuric acid in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ solution $=30 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ No. of moles of sulphuric acid $({\rm{n}}) = \frac{{\rm{m}}}{{\rm{M}}} = \frac{{30.0\,{\rm{g}}}}{{98\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}}$
$=0.306$
Molarity of sulphuric acid,
${\rm{M}} = \frac{{\rm{n}}}{{\rm{V}}} = \frac{{0.306\,{\rm{mol}}}}{{87.72 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,{\rm{L}}}} = 3.49\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{L}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The mole fraction of solute in an aqueous solvent is 0.1 . The molality of the solution is ____ m .
explanation:
${\mathrm{x_{\text {solvent }}=1-x_{\text {solute }}}}$$=1-0.1=0.9$
Assuming, moles of water ${\mathrm{=0.9}}$ and moles of solute ${\mathrm{=0.1}}$
Mass of water ${\mathrm{=0.9 \times 18=16.20 \mathrm{~g}=0.0162 \mathrm{~kg}}}$
Molality ${\mathrm{=\dfrac{\mathrm{mol} \text { solute }}{\text { Mass of solvent }(\mathrm{kg})}}}$$=\dfrac{0.1}{0.0162}=6.17 \mathrm{~m}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Normality of 0.25 M phosphorous acid $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$ is
explanation:
We know that,
Normality $=$ Molarity $\times$ Valency
Since phosphorous acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}\right)$ is dibasic thus, has valency 2 .
$\therefore$ Normality $=0.25 \mathrm{M} \times 2=0.50 \mathrm{~N}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 25 mL of a solution of barium hydroxide on titration with a 0.1 molar solution of hydrochloric acid gave a titre value of 35 mL . The molarity of barium hydroxide solution was
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\,\,\,{\rm{Ba}}{{({\rm{OH}})}_{\rm{2}}} + {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{HCl}} \to {\rm{BaC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}} + {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}\\ {{{\rm{M}}_1} = ?\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}} = 0.1{\rm{M}}}\\ {{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}} = 25\,{\rm{mL}}\,\,\,\,\,{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}} = 35\,{\rm{mL}}} \end{array}$
Applying,
$\,\,\,{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}} = {{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}$
$2 \times {{\rm{M}}_1} \times 25 = 1 \times 0.1 \times 35$
($\because $ Basicity of $\mathrm{HCl}=1$ and acidity of $\left.\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}=2\right)$
${{\text{M}}_{\text{1}}} = \frac{{1 \times 0.1 \times 35}}{{2 \times 25}} = 0.07\,\,{\text{M}}$
$\therefore$ Molarity of $\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, solution $=0.07 \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The equivalent weight of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ in
$2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is
subject: Chemistry

question: In redox reaction $1 \mathrm{~g}$-eq of reducing agent requires $\mathrm{P}$ gm-eq. of oxidising agent. The value of $\mathrm{P}$ is
explanation:
In redox reaction,
g equivalent of reducing agent= g.
equivalent of oxidising agent
Hence 1 g equ.of reducing agent = P g
equ. of oxidising agent.
subject: Chemistry

question: The number of molecules in $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is
explanation:
As, normality = molarity$ \times $ valence factor for ${{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4}$,
valence factor for ${{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4} = 2$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Normality}},{\rm{N = M}} \times {\rm{2}}$
${\rm{or}}\,{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{N}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}}}{{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.01M}}$
${\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
$ = \frac{{0.01 \times 100}}{{1000}} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}$ mol
$\left[ {\because {\text{Molarity = }}\frac{{{\text{Number}}\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{of}}\,\,{\text{moles}}\,{\text{ of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume}}\,\,{\text{in}}\,\,{\text{solution}}({\text{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{mL}})}} \times {\text{1000}}} \right]$
$\therefore $Number of molecules in 100mL of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}\,{\rm{N}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
$ = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{A}}}$
$ = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 6.023 \times {10^{23}}$
$ = 6.023 \times {10^{20}}{\rm{ molecules }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The normality of $10 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{V})$ of acetic acid is
explanation:
Normality$({\rm{N}}) = \frac{{{\rm{w}} \times 1000}}{{{\rm{M}} \times {\rm{V}}({\rm{mL}})}}$
$ = \frac{{10 \times 1000}}{{60 \times 100}} = 1.66$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{N}} = 1.66\,{\rm{N }} \Rightarrow {\rm{N}} \approx 1.7\,{\rm{N}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Calculate the molarity of one litre solution of $22.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The equivalent weight of a metal is 9 and vapour density of its chloride is 59.25.The atomic weight of metal is
explanation:
Given, equivalent weight of metal$ = 9$
Vapour density of metal chloride$ = 59.25$
$\therefore $ Molecular weight of metal chloride
${\rm{ = 2 }} \times {\rm{vapour\, density}}$
$ = 2 \times 59.25 = 118.50$
valency of metal ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{mol}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{.of}}\,{\rm{metal}}\,{\rm{chloride}}}}{{{\rm{eq}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{.of}}\,{\rm{metal + 35}}{\rm{.5}}}}$
Valency of metal $ = \frac{{118.5}}{{9 + 35.5}} = \frac{{118.5}}{{44.5}} = 2.66$
Therefore, atomic weight of the metal
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{equivalent}}\,{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{ \times valency}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 9 \times 2.66 = 23.9\,{\rm{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The formula mass of Mohr's salt is 392. The iron present in it is oxidised by $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ in acidic medium. The equivalent mass of Mohr's salt is
subject: Chemistry

question: $74.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of a metallic chloride contain $35.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of chlorine. The equivalent weight of the metal is
explanation:
Eq. wt. of metal
= $\frac{{{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{metal}}}}{{{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{chlorine}}\,{\rm{combined}}}} \times 35.5$
$\therefore {\rm{Eq}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{.of}}\,{\rm{metal}}
= \frac{{(74.5 - 35.5)}}{{35.5}} \times 35.5$
$ = 39.0{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{eq}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In an experiment, $4 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{x}}$ oxide was reduced to $2.8 \mathrm{~g}$ of the metal. If the atomic mass of the metal is $56 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the number of $\mathrm{O}$ - atoms in the oxide is
explanation:
Mass of oxygen in oxide
$ = 4 - 2.8 = 1.2\,{\rm{g}}$
Eq. wt. of metal
$ = \frac{{{\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal}}}}{{{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{oxygen}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{combined}}}} \times 8$
$\frac{{2.8}}{{1.2}} \times 8 = 18.67\,{\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{eq}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
Valency of metal
$ = \frac{{{\rm{atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{weight}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of }}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal}}}}{{{\rm{eq}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{.of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal}}}}$
$ = \frac{{{\rm{56}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(given)}}}}{{18.97}} = 2.99 \approx 3$
Hence, the number of O-atoms in the oxide is 3.
subject: Chemistry

question: When a metal is burnt, its weight is increased by 24 per cent. The equivalent weight of the metal will be
explanation:
If $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal is burnt, then it combines with oxygen to form metal oxide. As its weight increases by $24 \%$, then the mass of combined oxygen would be 24 g.
Thus, the equivalent weight of metal
= $\frac{{{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{metal}}}}{{{\rm{weight}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{oxygen}}\,{\rm{combined}}}} \times 8$
$ = \frac{{100}}{{24}} \times 8$
$ = 33.33\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{eq}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
Hence, the equivalent weight of metal will be 33.3.
subject: Chemistry

question: The equivalent weight of potassium permanganate in alkaline solution is equal to
explanation:
${\text{2K}}\mathop {{\text{Mn}}}\limits^{{\text{ + 7}}} {{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Alkaline}}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,2\mathop {{\text{Mn}}}\limits^{ + 4} {{\text{O}}_2}{\text{ + 2KOH + 3}}[{\text{O}}]$
${\rm{(As}}\,{\rm{change}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}\,{\rm{oxidation}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{number}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Mn}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{3)}}$
So, equivalent mass = $\frac{{{\rm{molar}}\,{\rm{mass}}}}{3}$
Hence, the equivalent weight of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
in alkaline solution is equal to$\frac{1}{3}{\rm{rd}}$ of the molar mass of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 50 mL of $20.8 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{V}) \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (aq) and 100 mL of $9.8 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{V}) \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq})$ solutions are mixed. Molarity of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions in the resulting solution is (At. wt. of $\mathrm{Ba}=137$ )
explanation:
$50\,\,{\text{ml}}\,\,20.8\% \,\,\frac{{\text{w}}}{{\text{v}}}\,{\text{ BaC}}{{\text{l}}_2}$ solution
${\text{M}} = \frac{{20.8}}{{208}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{50}} = 2$
$\therefore \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$concentration $=4 \mathrm{M}$. (As each molecule of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ has $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions)
from Dilution law :
$50 \times 2=150 \times \mathrm{M}_{2}$
$\mathrm{M}_{2}=0.666$
(Here no need of any reaction, Question is about chloride ion concentration only, which is diluted from 50 ml to 150 mL )

question: Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the given options given.
explanation:

${\text{N}} = 1.2 \times 3 = 3.6\,N$

question: 1 gram of sodium hydroxide was treated with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl solution, the mass of sodium hydroxide left unreacted is equal to
explanation:
$\text { mass }=\dfrac{\mathrm{M} \times \text { molar mass } \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{mL})}{1000}$
$=\dfrac{0.75 \times 36.5 \times 25}{1000}$
$=0.684 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{HCl}$
$\underset{36.5 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{HCl}}+\underset{40 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{NaOH}} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
36.5 g HCl reacts with 40 g NaOH.
0.684 g HCl reacts with ?
$=\dfrac{40}{36.5} \times 0.684 \simeq 0.750 \mathrm{~g}$
Amount of NaOH left $=1 \mathrm{~g}-0.750 \mathrm{~g}=0.250 \mathrm{~g}$
$=250\, \mathrm{mg}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 25 mL of silver nitrate solution ${\mathrm{\mathrm{(1 \mathrm{M})}}}$ is added dropwise to 25 mL of potassium iodide ( 1.05 ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{M})}}}$ solution. The ion(s) present in very small quantity in the solution is /are
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+\mathrm{KI} \rightarrow \mathrm{AgI} \downarrow+\mathrm{KNO}_{3}}}$
AgI is in an soluble salt so concentration of

question: The volume of 0.02 M aqueous HBr required to neutralize 10.0 mL of 0.01 M aqueous ${\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}}}$ is ____ .(Assume complete neutralization.)
explanation:
Neutralisation formula,
${\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}+2 \mathrm{HBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaBr}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{M}_{1}=0.01 \quad \mathrm{M}_{2}=0.02}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{V}_{1}=10 \mathrm{~mL} \quad \mathrm{~V}_{2}=}}$ ?
${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}_{1}=1 \quad \mathrm{n}_{2}=2}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{V}_{2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.01 \times 10}{1} \times \dfrac{2}{0.02}=10 \mathrm{~mL}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, in the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is $17 / 3$ while that of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is $28 / 6$.


Reason: Equivalent weight $=\dfrac{\text { Molecular weight }}{\text { Number of } \mathrm{e}^{-1} \text { lost }}$ or gained per mole
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}^{-3}$
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight of
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}=\dfrac{17}{3}$
(Mol. wt. of $\left.\mathrm{NH}_{3}=17\right)\left(3 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right.$change for each nitrogen atom)
While, equivalent weight of $\mathrm{N}_{2}=\dfrac{28}{6}$
( $6 \mathrm{e}^{-}$change for ' $2 \mathrm{~N}$ ' atoms)
So, the option (1) is correct.

explanation: Molality $(\mathrm{m})$ represents solute concentration in a solution, measured as moles of solute per kilogram of solvent, It is independent of temperature as it remains independent of solution volume, relying on the constant mass of the solvent. So, the option (1) is correct.

explanation: The equivalent weight of a compound can vary in different reactions, as it depends on the number of electrons lost or gained, influencing its ability to donate or accept one mole of electrons during a chemical reaction. So, the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{Cu}$ in $\mathrm{CuO}$ is 31.8 and in $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is 63.6.


Reason: Equivalent weight of an element
$=\dfrac{\text { Atomic weight }}{\mathrm{x}-\text { factor }}$
explanation:
The equivalent weight (eq. wt.) of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ in copper oxide $(\mathrm{CuO})$ is calculated as follows:The atomic weight (At.wt) of copper $(\mathrm{Cu})$ is $63.6 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, and its valency is 2 .
Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{Cu}$ in $\mathrm{CuO}=\dfrac{63.6}{2}=31.8$
Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{Cu}$ in $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\dfrac{63.6}{1}=63.6$
So, the option (1) is correct

question: What volume of ${\text{75}}\% \,\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ by mass is required to prepare 1.5 litres of ${\text{0}}{\text{.2}}\,{\text{M}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ ? (Density of the sample is $\mathrm{1.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}}$ )
explanation:
Mass of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$ needed to prepare $\mathrm{0.2 \mathrm{M}}$
$\mathrm{1.5 \mathrm{~L}}$ is Molarity $\mathrm{\times}$ gram molar mass $\mathrm{\times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{L})}$
$\mathrm{=0.2 \times 98 \times 1.5=29.4}$ gram $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}$
given density is $\mathrm{1.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}}$
So. volume needed is $\mathrm{\dfrac{29.4 \mathrm{~g}}{1.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}}=16.33 \mathrm{cc}}$
But it is $\mathrm{75 \%}$ only
So, volume needed of $\mathrm{75 \%}$ is
$\mathrm{\dfrac{16.33 \times 100}{75}=21.77 \mathrm{cc}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If the concentration of glucose $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)}$ in blood is $\mathrm{0.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$, what will be the molarity of glucose in blood?
explanation:
$\mathrm{0.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}$ means $\mathrm{1 \mathrm{~L}}$ solution contains $\mathrm{0.9 \mathrm{~g}}$ of glucose.
$\mathrm{M=\dfrac{\operatorname{mass}(\text { gram })}{\text { gram molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1}{V(L)}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.9}{180} \times \dfrac{1}{1}=0.005 \mathrm{M}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A metal oxide has the formula $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}$. It can be reduced by hydrogen to give free metal and water. $\mathrm{0.1596 \mathrm{~g}}$ of this metal oxide requires $\mathrm{6 \mathrm{mg}}$ of hydrogen for complete reduction. What is the atomic weight of metal?
explanation:
$\rm{\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~A}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\& 0.1596 \mathrm{~g} \quad 0.006 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}}$
$\mathrm{0.006 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ reduces $\mathrm{0.1596 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}$
$\mathrm{6 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$ reduces $\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.1596 \times 6}{0.006}}$
$\mathrm{=159.6 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}$.
Hence, molecular weight of $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}=159.6 \mathrm{~g}}$.
Let molecular weight of $\mathrm{\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 2 \mathrm{x}+3 \times 16=159.6}$
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{x}=159.6-48}$
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{x}=111.6}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{x}=\dfrac{111.6}{2}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{x}=55.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$.
Hence, atomic mass of metal $\mathrm{(\mathrm{A})=55.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{\mathrm{x}}$ moles of potassium dichromate oxidises 1 mole of ferrous oxalate in acidic medium. Here $\mathrm{\mathrm{x}}$ is
explanation:
The reaction of oxidation of ferrous oxalate by potassium dichromate in acidic medium is written as
${\rm{2Fe}}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ + 14}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}} \to $
${\rm{2F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + 2C}}{{\rm{r}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + 4C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
$\mathrm{\because 2}$ moles of $\mathrm{\mathrm{FeC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}$ are oxidised by $\mathrm{=1}$
mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}}$.
$\mathrm{\therefore 1}$ mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{FeC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}$ will be oxidised by
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5}$ mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Equvivalent weight of base ${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{weight}}}}{{{\text{acidity}}}}$

Reason: Acidity is the number of replaceable hydrogen atom in one molecule of the base
explanation:
Equivalent weight of a base is calculated by dividing its molecular mass with its acidity. Acidity is defined as the number of replaceable hydroxyl group in one molecule of the base.
Hence, Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: 1.25g of a solid dibasic acid is completely neutralised by 25 mL of 0.25 molar ${\rm{Ba(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{2}}}$ solution. Molecular mass of the acid is
explanation:
${\rm{(NV)}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\mkern 1mu} _{{\rm{Acid}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(NV)}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\mkern 1mu} _{{\rm{Base}}}}$
${{\rm{(Basicity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid \times MV)}}_{{\rm{Acid}}}}{\rm{ = (Acidity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{base)}}$
${\rm{ \times (MV}}{{\rm{)}}_{{\rm{Base}}}}$
${\rm{Basicity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid \times }}\frac{{{\rm{Given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}$
${\rm{ = Acidity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{base \times (MV}}{{\rm{)}}_{{\rm{base}}}}$
${\rm{2 \times }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.25}}}}{{{\rm{molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{\rm{ = 2 \times 0}}{\rm{.25 \times 0}}{\rm{.025}}$
${\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass = 200}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g/mol}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: When the solute is present in trace quantities the following expression is used?
explanation:
When the solute is present in trace quantities it is convenient to express the concentration in parts per million (ppm).
It is defined as the quantity of the solute in grams present in ${\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{6}}}$ grams of the solutions.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Choose the concentration term which is temperature independent.
I. Mass percentage II. Ppm
III. Mole fraction IV. Molarity
explanation: Mass precentage, ppm, mole fraction and molality are independent of temperature whereas molarity is a function of temperature. This is because volume depends on temperature and the mass does not.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Two binary solutions have the same molarity.
Which of the following statements is true?
explanation:
${\rm{Molarity = }}\frac{{\rm{w}}}{{{\rm{MW \times V}}}}$
For two solutions having molarity,
$\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
${\rm{If}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
Thus, solutions contain equal number of moles of solute and hence equal number of molecules.

options.text: Equal volumes of the two solutions contain equal number of solute molecules.
Equal weights of the two solutions contain equal number of solute molecules.
The two solutions must have the same molality
The two solutions must have the same mole fraction.

subject: Chemistry

question: 250 mL of a sodium carbonate solution contains 2.65 grams of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$. If 10 mL of this solution is diluted to one litre, what is the concentration of the resultant solution? (molecular weight of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ = 106 g/mol)
explanation:
${\rm{M = }}\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{{\rm{W}}_{{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}}}{\rm{ \times V(mL)}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.65 \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{106 \times 250}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.1M}}$
When 10 mL of this solution is diluted to 1 L i.e., 1000 mL, concentration decreases by 100 times
${\rm{i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{.,}}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}{\rm{M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(or)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}{\rm{.001}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M}}$
subject: Chemistry

subject: Chemistry

question:
Three statements are given about mole fraction
i) Mole fraction of a solute + mole fraction of solvent = 1
ii) Equal weights of Helium and methane are present in a gaseous mixture. The mole fraction of He is $\frac{{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{5}}}$
iii) The mole fraction of 40% by mass of NaOH solution is 0.5
explanation:
(i) Sum of the mole fraction of all the components in solution is 1.
(ii) Let us assume weight of He and ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ as 1 g each.
Mole fraction of ${\rm{He = }}\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{He}}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{He}}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}}}{{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{16}}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{5}}}$
(iii) 40% by mass of NaOH means 40 g of NaOH present in 100 g of solution or 60 g of water.
Mole fraction of NaOH
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{NaOH}}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{{\rm{NaOH}}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( {\frac{{{\rm{40}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\rm{40}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}} \right){\rm{ + }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{60}}}}{{{\rm{18}}}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.23}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
In the reaction
${\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}} \to {\rm{NaBr + NaBr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
The equivalent weight of ${\rm{NaBr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is
explanation:
Valency factor for formation of ${\rm{NaBr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 5}}$
$\therefore $ Equivalent weight of ${\rm{NaBr}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mol}}{\rm{.wt}}}}{{\rm{5}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: An element forms an oxide in which the oxygen is 20% of the oxide by weight, the equivalent weight of the given element will be
explanation:
${{\rm{a}}_{\rm{1}}}$ gm of the metal gave ${{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}$ gm of its oxide.
Hence equivalent weight of the metal is $\frac{{{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ \times 8}}$
According to the given data, equivalent weight of metal is $\frac{{{\rm{80}}}}{{{\rm{100 - 80}}}}{\rm{ \times 8 = 32}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: One gram of hydrogen is found to combine with 80 g of bromine. One gram of calcium (valency = 2) combines with 4 g of bromine. The equivalent weight of calcium is
explanation:
As 1 g ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ combines with 80 g ${\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}$, eq. wt. of ${\rm{Br = 80}}$.
4 g ${\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}$ combine with 1 g Ca
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{80}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}$ combine with Ca = 20 g
Hence eq. wt. of Ca = 20.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following statement is true?
explanation: The unit of molality is mol/kg

options.text: The unit of molality is mol/kg.
The unit of molarity is mol/kg.
1 molal means one mole of solute is present in 1 L of solvent.
1 molar means one mole of solute is present in 1 kg of solvent.

subject: Chemistry

question: In which mode of expression, the concentration of a solution is independent of temperature?
explanation:
Molality is the one which depends upon the mass of the solute and the solvent. The mass is independent of temperature. Thus, molality is independent of temperature.
Molarity, Normality and Formality concentration terms are inversely proportional to volume which is directly proportional to the temperature. So, they are depends on temperature.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following has no units?
explanation: Mole fraction is the ratio of moles of substance so units cancels out.

question: An X molal solution of a compound in benzene has mole fraction of solute = 0.2. The value of X is

Mole fraction of solute, ${{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2}}$.
Therefore, mole fraction of solvent, ${{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}$
$\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}$
$\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.8}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}$
Now, if ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}$ (solvent moles) ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1000}}}}{{{\rm{78}}}}{\rm{ = 12}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$
${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{12}}{\rm{.8}}}}{{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = 3}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$. Therefore, 3.2 moles of the compound are present in one Kg of solvent benzene and so molality = 3.2 m.

question: 6.90 gm of a metal carbonate were dissolved in 60 mL of 2N HCl. The excess acid was neutralised by 20 mL of 1N NaOH. What is the equivalent wt. of metal ?
explanation:

Equivalents of HCl present after the reaction ${\rm{ = 20 \times 1 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$
$\therefore $ Equivalents of HCl utilised ${\rm{ = 100 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{100 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$ equivalents of metal carbonate ${\rm{ = 6}}{\rm{.90}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{gm}}$
$\therefore 1$ equivalents of metal carbonate ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{6}}{\rm{.90}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}{\rm{ = 69 gm}}$
$\therefore $ Equivalent weight of metal = 69 – 30 = 39
[because equivalent weight of carbonate = 30]

question: 3 g of activated charcoal was added to 50 mL of acetic acid solution 0.06N in a flask. After an hour it was filtered and the strength of the filtrate was found to be 0.042 N. The amount of acetic acid adsorbed (per gram of charcoal) is:
explanation:
Let the weight of acetic acid initially be ${{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}$ in 50 mL of 0.060 N solution.
Let the ${\rm{N = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{. \times 50}}}}$
${\rm{(Normality = 0}}{\rm{.06N)}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.06 = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{60 \times 50}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.06 \times 60 \times 50}}}}{{{\rm{1000}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.18g = 180}}\,\,{\rm{mg}}$
After an hour, the strength of acetic acid = 0.042 N
so, let the weight of acetic acid be ${{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}$
${\rm{N = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{60 \times 50}}}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.042 = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{3000}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.126 g = 126 mg}}$
So amount of acetic acid adsorbed per 3 g = 180 - 126 mg = 54 mg
$\therefore $ Amount of acetic acid absorbed per
${\rm{g = }}\frac{{{\rm{54}}}}{{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 18g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
3.92 g/L of a sample of ferrous ammonium sulphate reacts completely with 50 mL.
$\frac{{\rm{N}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ solution. The percentage purity of the sample is:
explanation:
${{\rm{(NV)}}_{{\rm{salt}}}}{\rm{ = (NV}}{{\rm{)}}_{{\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}}}$
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ \times 1000 = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{ \times 50}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{200}}}}$
${\rm{Eq}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{.of}}$
${\rm{FeS}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{(N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{)}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.6}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O = Mol}}{\rm{.wt}}{\rm{. = 392}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Strength}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{pure}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{salt = 392 \times }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{200}}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.96g}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\% purity = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.96}}}}{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.92}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 50\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 3.92 g of ferrous ammonium sulphate crystals are dissolved in 100 mL of water. 20 ml of this solution requires 18 mL of potassium permanganate during titration for complete oxidation. The weight of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ present in one litre of the solution of
explanation:
Eq . wt of ferrous ammonium sulpate = 392
$\therefore $ Normality of salt sol.
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.92}}}}{{{\rm{392}}}}{\rm{ \times }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ \times 1000 = 0}}{\rm{.1N}}$
20 mL of 0.1 N salt sol. $ \equiv {\rm{18}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ml}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{pf}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{sol}}$
$\therefore $ Normality of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ sol ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{20 \times 0}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{9}}}{\rm{N}}$
Eq. wt. of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = 31}}{\rm{.6}}$
$\therefore $ Strength of ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{sol = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{9}}}{\rm{ \times 31}}{\rm{.6 = 3}}{\rm{.5g}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ approx.
subject: Chemistry

question:
10 mL of 0.02 M ${\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is required to oxidise 20 ml of oxalic acid of certain strength. 25 ml of the same oxalic acid is required to neutralise 20 ml of NaOH of unknown strength. Find the amount of NaOH in a litre of the solution
(Molecular weight of NaOH = 40):
explanation:
In acidic medium,
${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = 5N}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.1N}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
According to normality equation,
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{(KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{) = }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(Oxalic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{acid)}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1 \times 10 = }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 20}}$
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10 \times 0}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{20}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.05N}}$
Again ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}$ (Oxalic acid) ${\rm{ = }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{NaOH}}} \right)$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.05 \times 25 = }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 20}}$
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.05 \times 25}}}}{{{\rm{20}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.0625N}}$
${\rm{As}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{S = N \times E}}$
${\rm{So}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{S = 0}}{\rm{.0625 \times 40 = 2}}{\rm{.5g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving 120g of urea (mol. mass = 60u ) in 1000g of water is 1.15 g/mL. The molarity of this solution is
explanation:
Total mass of solution = 1000 g water + 120 g urea = 1120 g
Thus, volume of solution
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{density}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1120}}\,\,{\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.15}}\,\,{\rm{g/mL}}}}$
${\rm{ = 973}}{\rm{.91}}\,\,{\rm{mL = 0}}{\rm{.974}}\,\,{\rm{L}}$
${\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute = }}\frac{{{\rm{120}}}}{{{\rm{60}}}}{\rm{ = 2mol}}$
${\rm{Molarity = }}\frac{{{\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{volume(L)of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.974}}\,\,{\rm{L}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.05}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

${\rm{Molality = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.01}}}}{{{\rm{60 \times 0}}{\rm{.3}}}}{\rm{ = 5}}{\rm{.55 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 4}}}}{\rm{M}}$

question: 5.47 g of Mg metal reacted completely with 14.31 g of a non metal. The compound of this non metal with hydrogen contains 3.1% H. The equivalent weight of Mg is
explanation:
Weight of Mg = 5.47 g
Weight of non metal = 14.31 g
Weight of non metal in hydrogen compound = 100 – 3.1 = 96.9
14.31 g of non metal contains
$\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{96}}{\rm{.9}}}}{\rm{ \times 14}}{\rm{.31 = 0}}{\rm{.4578}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
Equivalent of Mg ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{5}}{\rm{.47}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.4578}}}}{\rm{ \times 1 = 11}}{\rm{.95}}$

question: One gram of a metal chloride was found to contain 0.835 g of chlorine. Its vapour density is 85. The molecular formula of metal chloride is
explanation:
Mass of metal chloride = 1g
Mass of chlorine = 0.835 g
Mass of metal = (1 – 0.835) = 0.165 g
Equivalent mass of metal ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.165 \times 35}}{\rm{.5}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.835}}}}{\rm{ = 7}}{\rm{.01}}$
Valency of the metal
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2V}}{\rm{.D}}}}{{{\rm{E + 35}}{\rm{.5}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2 \times 85}}}}{{{\rm{7}}{\rm{.01 + 35}}{\rm{.5}}}}{\rm{ = 4}}$
Formula of the chloride ${\rm{ = MC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{4}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The density of 3M solution of sodium chloride is ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.252g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{m}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. The molality of the solution will be: (molar mass of, ${\rm{58}}{\rm{.5}}\,\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$)
explanation:
The relation between molarity (M) and molality (m) is $d = M\left( {\frac{1}{m} + \frac{{{M_2}}}{{1000}}} \right),\,\,{M_2} = Mol$. mass of solute On substituting value
$1.252 = 3\left( {\frac{1}{m} + \frac{{58.5}}{{1000}}} \right)$
on solving $m = 2.79m$

question: Dissolving 120 g of a compound of molar mass 60 in 1000 g of water gave a solution of density 1.12 g/mL. The molarity of the solution is:
explanation:
Given
Mass of solute $({w_2}) = 120\,\,g$
Mass of solvent $({w_1}) = 1000\,\,g$
Mol. mass of solute $ = 60\,\,g\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}}$
Density of solution $ = 1.12\,\,g/ml$
From the given data,
Mass of solution $ = 1000 + 120 = 1 = 120\,\,g$
Volume of the solution, $V = \frac{{Mass\,\,of\,\,the\,\,solution}}{{Density\,\,of\,\,the\,\,solution}}$
$V = \frac{{1120}}{{60 \times 1}} = 1000\,\,mL = 1L$
Now, Molarity $(M) = \frac{{{W_2}}}{{Mol \cdot mass \times V(lit)}}$
$ = \frac{{120}}{{60 \times 1}} = 2M$
subject: Chemistry

question: The volume of 0.1 N dibasic acid sufficient to neutralise 1 g of a base that gives 0.04 mole of ${\rm{O}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}}$ in aqueous solution:
explanation:
Applying law of equivalence,
Equivalence of acid = Equivalence of base
${(NV)_{acid}} = {(n \times ch\arg e)_{base}}$
$0.1 \times V = 0.04 \times 1$
$V = 0.4L = 0.4 \times 1000 = 400mL$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Excess of NaOH (aq) was added to 100 mL of ${\rm{FeC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(aq)}}$ resulting into 2.14 g of ${\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}$. The molarity of ${\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is :
(Given molar mass of ${\rm{Fe = 56}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ and molar mass of ${\rm{Cl = 35}}{\rm{.5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$)
explanation:
$\mathop {{\rm{FeC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(aq)}}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{limiting}}}\\{{\rm{reagent}}}\end{array}} {\rm{ + }}\mathop {{\rm{3NaOH(aq)}}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{(Excessamount)}}}\\{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{Notbehaveas}}}\\{{\rm{limiting}}\,{\rm{reagent}}}\end{array}}\end{array}} $
${\rm{Moles}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{weighting}}}}{{{\rm{M}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{weight}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.14}}\,\,{\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{107}}\,\,{\rm{g}}\,\,{\rm{/}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}}}{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.02}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}$
1.0 mole of ${\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is obtained from 1.0 mole of ${\rm{FeC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}$
0.02 mole of ${\rm{Fe(OH}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{3}}}$ will be obtained from 0.02 mole ${\rm{FeC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{Molarity = }}\frac{{{\rm{No}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}}}{{{\rm{Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.02mole}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.1L}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2M}}$
subject: Chemistry
