question: 5.0 g of sodium hydroxide (molar mass ${\rm{40}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$) is dissolved in little quantity of water and the solution is diluted upto 100 mL. What is the molarity of the resulting solution?
explanation:
Given, W (mass of solute) = 5.0 g
m (molar mass of solute) ${\rm{ = 40}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
Molarity is given as,
${\rm{M = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L)}}}}$
${\rm{M = }}\frac{{{\rm{W \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{m \times Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mL)}}}}$
${\rm{M = }}\frac{{{\rm{5}}{\rm{.0}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{40 \times 100}}}}$
${\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: ${\rm{50}}\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}{\rm{.04M}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}}$ in acidic medium oxidizes a sample of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}$ gas to sulphur. Volume of ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.03}}\,\,{\rm{M}}\,\,{\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ required to oxidize the same amount of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}$  gas to sulphur, in acidic medium is
explanation:
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
both are good oxidizing agents in acidic medium. The reaction is as follows:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ + 14}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {\rm{2C}}{{\rm{r}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + 7}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
${\rm{MnO}}_{\rm{4}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ + 8}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + 5}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {\rm{M}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}{\rm{ + 4}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
Normality of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ = Molarity \times no}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electrons}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{involved}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.04 \times 6 = 0}}{\rm{.24N}}$
Normality of ${\rm{MnO}}_{\rm{4}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ = Molarity \times no}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electrons}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{involved}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.03 \times 5 = 0}}{\rm{.15N}}$
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}$
$\left( {{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}}} \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{\rm{KMn}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{0}}{\rm{.24 \times 50 = 0}}{\rm{.15 \times }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}$
${{\rm{V}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.24 \times 50}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.15}}}}{\rm{ = 80}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many grams of concentrated nitric acid solution should be used to prepare 250 mL of 2.0 M ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ ? The concentrated acid is 70% ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ :
explanation:
1000 mL has 2 moles
250 mL has $\frac{{\rm{2}}}{{{\rm{1000}}}}{\rm{ \times 250 = 0}}{\rm{.5}}\,\,{\rm{moles}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{n = }}\frac{{\rm{W}}}{{\rm{M}}} \Rightarrow {\rm{W = n \times M = 0}}{\rm{.5 \times 63g}}$
As 70 g of ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is in 100 g solution
So, ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.5 \times 63}}\,\,{\rm{g}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is in $\frac{{{\rm{100}}}}{{\rm{7}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.5 \times 63 = 45g}}$
[We have assumed 70% (mass by mass), although it should have been reported]
subject: Chemistry

question: ${\rm{25}}\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of oxalic acid completely neutralised 0.064 g of sodium hydroxide. Molarity of the oxalic acid solution is
explanation:
${\left( {{\rm{COOH}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 2NaOH}} \to {\left( {{\rm{COONa}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mole}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mole}}$
${\rm{Mol}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{NaOH = 40}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
No. of g moles in 0.064 g of NaOH ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.064}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.0016}}$
No. of moles of oxalic acid ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.0016}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 4}}}}$
Volume of solution (in L) ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{25}}}}{{{\rm{1000}}}}$
Hence, molarity ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{No}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L)}}}}$
${\rm{ = 8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 4}}}}{\rm{ \times }}\frac{{{\rm{1000}}}}{{{\rm{25}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.032M}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 100 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid is completely neutralized using a standard solution of NaOH. The volume of ethane obtained at STP after the complete electrolysis of the resulting solution is
explanation:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COOH + NaOH}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}$
${\rm{2C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} \to $
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ - C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ + 2C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 2NaOH + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ Number of moles of acetic acid = Number of moles of sodium acetate = ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1 \times 100 = 10}}\,{\rm{m}}$
moles = ${\rm{10 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}\,\,{\rm{moles}}$
2 moles of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}$ on electrolysis produces 22400 mL of ethane.
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{10 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$ of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COONa}}$ will produce
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{22400 \times 10 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 112}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mL}}$ of ethane.
subject: Chemistry

question: The equivalent mass of a certain bivalent metal is 20. The molecular mass of its anhydrous chloride is
explanation:
Let the compound be ${\rm{MC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$
Equivalent mass of a metal ion ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{positive}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{valency}}}}$
${\rm{20 = }}\frac{{{\rm{Atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ion}}}}{{\rm{2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Atomic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{the}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal = 2 \times 20 = 40}}$
$\therefore $ Molecular mass of anhydrous chloride
${\rm{(MC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{)}}$
${\rm{ = 40 + (2 \times 35}}{\rm{.5) = 40 + 71 = 111}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Molarity is
explanation:
Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre or one ${\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ volume of solution. It is expressed by the symbol ‘M’
${\rm{Molarity(M) = }}\frac{{{\rm{Number}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{)}}}}$

options.text: The number of moles of solute present in ${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ volume of solution
The number of moles of solute dissolved in 1 kg of solvent
The number of moles of solute dissolved in 1 kg of solution
The number of moles of solute dissolved in ${\rm{100}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ volume of solution

subject: Chemistry

question: The molarity of urea (molar mass 60 g ${\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$) solution by dissolving 15 g of urea in ${\rm{500}}\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of water is
explanation:
Given, mass of urea = 15 g
Molar mass of urea ${\rm{ = 60gmo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
Volume ${\rm{ = 500c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 500mL = 0}}{\rm{.5L}}$
$\because {\text{1c}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}{\text{ = 1mL 1L = 1000mL}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Molarity}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{grams(w)}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute(M) \times Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V(L)}}}}$
${\rm{Molarity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{urea = }}\frac{{{\rm{15g}}}}{{{\rm{60g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.5L}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}{\rm{.5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol/L = 0}}{\rm{.5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol/d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
$\left( {{\rm{1L = 1d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}} \right)$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the molarity of solution containing 3.2 g of NaOH (molar mass 40 g ${\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$) in ${\rm{250}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of water?
explanation:
Given, mass of NaOH = 3.2 g Molecular
mass of NaOH = 40 g ${\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ volume of solution ${\rm{(V) = 250}}\,\,{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.25}}\,\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$$\therefore {\rm{Molarity = }}\frac{{{\rm{Given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{NaOH}}}}{{{\rm{Molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{NaOH \times Volume}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.2g}}}}{{{\rm{40}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.32}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
${\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COOH}}$ dissolved in ${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ solvent, what is molality of solution?
$\left( {{\rm{density = 1}}{\rm{.25g/c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given,
${\rm{Volume = 1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{Density = 1}}{\rm{.25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g/c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{Mass = 60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
We know that, molality
${\rm{(M) = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solvents}}}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Moles = }}\frac{{{\rm{Given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g//mol}}}}$
${\rm{ = 1}}\,{\rm{mol}}$
${\rm{Molarity(m) = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}}}{{{\rm{Volum}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{Density}}}}$
${\rm{m = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1000}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\mkern 1mu} ^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{\rm{.25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g/c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2}}{{\rm{5}}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{m}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The molality of solution containing 15.20 g of urea, (molar mass = 60) dissolved in 150 g of water is
explanation:
Molality
${\rm{(m) = }}\frac{{{\rm{Number}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solvent(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g)}}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g) \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{Molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{weight}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}}}$
${\rm{ \times }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solvent}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g)}}$
$ = \frac{{{W_{solute}} \times 1000}}{{{M_{solute}} \times {W_{solvent}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{15}}{\rm{.2 \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{60 \times 150}}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.689mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{k}}{{\rm{g}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The volume of 10 N and 4 N HCl required to make 1 litre of 7 N HCl are
explanation:
Let V lit 10 N HCl is mixed with ${\rm{(1 - )}}$ lit 4 NHCl
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{10XV + (1 - V)4 = 1 \times 7}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{V = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.5}}$
$\therefore {\rm{0}}{\rm{.54}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{it}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{10}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{NHCl + 0}}{\rm{.54}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{it}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4NHCl}}$
$ \equiv {\rm{12}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{lit}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{7NHCl}}$

options.text: 0.50 litre of 10 N HCl and 0.50 litre of 4 N HCl
0.60 litre of 10 N HCl and 0.40 litre of 4 N HCl
0.80 litre of 10 N HCl and 0.20 litre of 4 N HCl
0.75 litre of 10 N HCl and 0.25 litre of 4 N HCl

subject: Chemistry

question: 1 M HCl and 2 M HCl are mixed in volume ratio of 4 : 1. What is the final molarity of HCl solution?
explanation: $\left[ {{\rm{HCl}}} \right]{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1 \times 4V + 2 \times V}}}}{{{\rm{5V}}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.2M}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The mass of a metal, with equivalent mass 31.75 which would combine with 8 g of oxygen is
explanation:
Equivalent mass of metal = 31.75
Mass of metal = x
Combine with 8 g of oxygen
${\rm{Equivalent}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal = }}\frac{{{\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{metal}}}}{{{\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{oxygen}}}}{\rm{ \times 8}}$
i.e., ${\rm{31}}{\rm{.75 = }}\frac{{\rm{x}}}{{\rm{8}}}{\rm{ \times 8}} \Rightarrow {\rm{x = 31}}{\rm{.75}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: ${\rm{10}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of 0.1 N monobasic acid requires ${\rm{15}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of sodium hydroxide solution whose normality is
subject: Chemistry

question: Molarity of a given orthophosphoric acid solution is 3 M. Its normality is
explanation:
Molarity of orthophosphoric acid = 3M
Now, basicity of orthophosphoric acid ${\rm{(}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{) = 3}}$
$\therefore $ Normality of orthophosphoric acid
${\rm{ = 3 \times 3N = 9N}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A metallic chloride contain 47.22% metal. Calculate the equivalent weight of metal
explanation:
Suppose weight of metallic chloride =100 g
Then weight of metal = 47.22 g
Weight of chlorine = 100 – 47.22 = 52.78 g
$\therefore $ Equivalent weight of metal = $\left( {\frac{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Metal}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{chloride}}}}} \right){\rm{ \times 35}}{\rm{.5}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{47}}{\rm{.22}}}}{{{\rm{52}}{\rm{.78}}}}{\rm{ \times 35}}{\rm{.5 = 31}}{\rm{.76}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following terms with their definitions. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
(A - P) The limiting reagent is the one that limits the amount of product formation and gets used up first.
(B - Q) The law of multiple proportions indicates that when two elements from more than one compound, the masses of one element combine with a fixed mass of the other is in a ratio of small whole numbers.
(C - S) The law of definite proportions states that a chemical compound always contains its elements in a fixed ratio, and that ratio is unique for each compound.
(D - R) The law of conservation of mass expresses that the mass remains constant during a reaction. Correct option is (2).

options.text: A - R,     B - S, 	C - Q, 	D - P
A - P,      B - Q, 	C - S, 	D - R
A - S,      B - R, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - Q,      B - P, 	C - R, 	D - S

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The limiting reagent determines the maximum amount of product that can be formed in a chemical reaction.
The reactant that is present in excess is always left over after the reaction is complete.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because the limiting reagent is consumed first and thus limits the formation of the product. Statement B is also true because the excess reactant will not be entirely consumed when the limiting reagent is spent. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Stoichiometry is only used to determine the amounts of reactants and products in chemical reactions.
Stoichiometry also involves the study of energy changes during chemical reactions.
explanation: Statement A is true as stoichiometry deals with the calculations involving the masses or volumes of reactants and products. Statement B is false because stoichiometry does not concern itself with energy changes; thermodynamics covers that aspect. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A balanced equation is necessary for performing stoichiometric calculations.
The coefficients in a balanced equation represent the mass ratios of reactants and products.
explanation: Statement A  is true as stoichiometric calculations are based on the relative amounts of reactants and products, which are represented by their coefficients in a balanced chemical equation. Statement B is false because the coefficients represent mole ratios, not mass ratios. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
In a reaction where nitrogen gas reacts with hydrogen gas to form ammonia, the volume ratio of nitrogen to hydrogen is 1:3.
For gaseous reactions at constant temperature and pressure, the ratio of the volumes of reactants and products is equal to the stoichiometric coefficients from the balanced equation.
explanation: Both statements are true. The reaction between nitrogen and hydrogen to form ammonia has a volume ratio of 1:3 as per the balanced chemical equation  ${{\text{N}}_2} + 3{{\text{H}}_2} \to 2{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3},$  and this ratio corresponds to the stoichiometric  coefficients in the equation, affirming  Statement B. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The volume of one mole of any gas at standard temperature and pressure is 22.4 liters.
The volume of gases involved in a reaction at standard temperature and pressure cannot be used to determine the stoichiometry of a reaction.
explanation: Statement A is true because, according to Avogadro's law, one mole of any gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 liters. Statement B is false because the volumes of gases at STP can indeed be used to determine the stoichiometry of a reaction. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
In stoichiometry, the amount of product obtained is directly proportional to the amount of the limiting reagent.
If a reaction is carried out with non-stoichiometric amounts of reactants, the product obtained is independent of the amounts of reactants used.
explanation: Statement A is true because the amount of product formed in a reaction is indeed determined by the amount of the limiting reagent. Statement B is false, as the amount of product formed in a non-stoichiometric reaction still depends on the amounts of the reactants, particularly the limiting reagent. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A chemical reaction that has occurred can be reversed completely by adding more products.
The conversion of reactants into products in a chemical reaction is always 100% efficient.
explanation: Statement A is false because not all reactions are reversible, and even if they are, simply  adding more products does not guarantee the reaction will reverse completely. Statement B is false because many reactions do not proceed to (100 %) completion due to equilibrium or kinetic constraints. Correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The coefficients in a balanced chemical equation represent the relative volumes of reactants and products if all are gases.
The relative volumes of gases participating in a chemical reaction can be different from the stoichiometric coefficients when measured at the same conditions of temperature and pressure.
explanation: Statement A is true because, according to Avogadro's law, equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules, and the stoichiometric coefficients can represent the ratio of volumes of gaseous reactants and products. Statement B is false as the stoichiometric coefficients indeed represent the relative volumes of reactants and products in a gas phase reaction under the same conditions. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
The limiting reagent in a chemical reaction is the one that is completely consumed first.
The excess reagent in a reaction is the one that determines the amount of product formed.
explanation: Statement  A  is correct as the limiting reagent is the one that gets consumed first and thus limits the reaction. Statement  B  is incorrect because it is the limiting reagent, not the excess reagent, that determines the maximum amount of product formed.Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
In stoichiometric calculations, if the quantities of the reactants are given, one can predict the amount of products formed.
Stoichiometry is only concerned with the reactants in a chemical reaction, not the products.
explanation: Statement A is true as stoichiometry involves calculating the amounts of both reactants and products based on a balanced chemical equation. Statement B is false because stoichiometry concerns both reactants and products.  Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: Stoichiometry can involve calculations with both masses and volumes of reactants and products.

Reason: Stoichiometry is based on the assumption that reactants and products in a chemical reaction have the same volume.
explanation: Stoichiometry does involve calculations of masses and volumes; however, the Reason is false as stoichiometry does not assume equal volumes for reactants and products but is based on the mole concept. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: In stoichiometric calculations, it's possible to encounter a situation where a reactant is not completely consumed.

Reason: The reactant that is not completely consumed is called the excess reagent.
explanation: While the Assertion is true as a reactant may not be completely consumed, the Reason incorrectly describes the excess reagent. The term 'limiting reagent' refers to the reactant that is completely consumed first, limiting the amount of product formed. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: A balanced chemical equation has the same number of atoms of each element on both sides.

Reason: This is because the law of conservation of mass states that mass can neither be created nor destroyed.
explanation: The law of conservation of mass dictates that the mass remains constant in a chemical reaction, which implies that atoms are neither created nor destroyed, leading to a balanced chemical equation. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: The masses of carbon dioxide and water (in g) respectively formed during complete combustion of 10 g of glucose at STP are
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}+6 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
180 g glucose gives $6 \times 44 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
10 g glucose gives?
$=\dfrac{10 \times 6 \times 44}{180}=14.66 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
180 g glucose gives $6 \times 18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
10 g glucose gives?
$ = \frac{{10 \times 6 \times 18}}{{180}} = 6{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Calculate the number of moles of NaOH required to completely neutralise 100 g of 118 % oleum.
subject: Chemistry

question: The amount of sucrose needed to produce 1 mole of glucose using acid hydrolysis is
explanation:
1 mole of sucrose on acid hydrolysis produces 1 mole of glucose and 1 mole of fructose.
$\underset{\text { Sucrose }}{\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}} \xrightarrow{\text { Acid hydrolysis }} \underset{\text { Glucose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}+\underset{\text { Fructose }}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}}$
Molecular mass of sucrose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}\right)$ is $342 \mathrm{~g} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$.
Therefore, amount of sucrose needed to produce 1 mole of glucose using acid hydrolysis is 342 g.
subject: Chemistry

question: Magnetite can be reduced with CO to yield iron metal and carbon dioxide. Calculate the mass of magnetite (in kg) needed to obtain 4 kg of iron if the process is $80 \%$ efficient. [Atomic weight of Fe and O are 56 g and 16 g , respectively]
explanation:
Balanced chemical equation can be given as
$\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+4 \mathrm{CO} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Fe}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$
1 mol of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}(232 \mathrm{~g})$ produces 3 mole $\mathrm{Fe}(168 \mathrm{~g})$
[Molecular mass of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}=232 \mathrm{~g}$ ]
Amount of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ required for 4 kg iron
$=\dfrac{232 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}}{168 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}} \times 4=5.52 \mathrm{~kg}$
Since, efficiency of reaction is $80 \%$. Hence, required amount of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will be
$=\dfrac{100 \times 5.52}{80} \mathrm{~kg}=6.9 \mathrm{~kg}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Chlorine is allowed to react with excess of ammonia. In this, 1 mole of chlorine can oxidise ' $Z$ ' moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. ' $Z$ ' is
explanation:
The balanced reaction is
$3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+8 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$
If 3 moles of chlorine is reacting with 8 moles of ammonia.
$\therefore 1$ mole of chlorine will react with $8 / 3$ moles of ammonia.
subject: Chemistry

question: During the process of fermentation, the number of moles of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ liberated from one mole of glucose is
explanation:
Two molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ are produced from 1 mole of glucose during fermentation process. Chemical reaction is as follows:
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}(\mathrm{aq}) \xrightarrow{\text { Yeast }} 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})$
subject: Chemistry

question: 56 g of CaO has been mixed with 63 g of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, the amount of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ formed is
explanation:
The reaction is as follows
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{CaO}} + 2{\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_3} \to }&{{\text{Ca}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right)}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \\ {}&{56\,{\text{g}}\quad \,126\,{\text{g}}}&{164\,{\text{g}}} \\ {{\text{Given}},}&{56\,{\text{g}}\quad \,63\,{\text{g}}}&? \end{array}$
According to balanced equation, 1 mole of CaO reacts with 2 moles of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ to give 1 mole of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$. But the given amount of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, is exactly half, i.e. $1 / 2$ mole. In this reaction, $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$, acts as a limiting reagent. As 126 g ( 2 moles) of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ gives 164 g $(1 \mathrm{~mol})$ of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
So, $56 \mathrm{~g}(1 \mathrm{~mol})$ of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ of gives $\dfrac{164}{2} \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}=82 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Sulphuric acid reacts with sodium hydroxide as follows:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
What will be the amount of sodium sulphate formed, when 1 L of 0.2 M sulphuric acid is allowed to react with 1 L of 0.2 M sodium hydroxide solution?
explanation:
1 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ needs 2 moles NaOH .
0.2 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (given) needs 0.4 mole NaOH . But only 0.2 mole NaOH is given, so NaOH is limiting reagent.
2 mole NaOH gives 1 mole $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (142g)
0.2 mole NaOH gives?
$=\dfrac{0.2 \times 142}{2}=14.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: How many moles of ammonia are produced by 5 moles of hydrogen? [EAMCET 2020]
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
3 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gives 2 moles $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
5 mole $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gives $\dfrac{5 \times 2}{3}=\dfrac{10}{3}=3.3$ moles $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ Hence, correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question:
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ can react with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in presence of a catalyst to form $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ as per the following reaction,
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\text { Catalyst }} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})$
The amount of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ in grams required to produce 50 grams of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ is
subject: Chemistry

question: How many grams of $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ is needed to get 1 gram of copper? (Atomic mass: $\mathrm{Cu}=$ 63.5, $\mathrm{N}=14, \mathrm{O}=16$ )
explanation:
63.5 g of copper will get by 187.5 g of $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper will get by $\dfrac{187}{63.5}=2.95 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question:
For a given unbalanced reaction,
$\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$, which is the limiting reagent, if the initial amount for each of the reactant is 100 grams?
[Molar masses: $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}=86.9 ; \mathrm{HCl}=36.5$;
$\left.\mathrm{MnCl}_{2}=125.8 ; \mathrm{Cl}_{2}=70.9 ; \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=18\right]$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$, which is required to produce 10 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ in the following reaction is
\begin{equation*}\mathrm{aH}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{bNO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{cNH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{dH}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g})\end{equation*}
explanation:
The balanced equation is
$7 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
2 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is obtained from 7 moles $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
10 moles of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is obtained from $=\dfrac{10 \times 7}{2}=$
35 moles $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
$=\dfrac{10 \times 7}{2}=35$ moles $\mathrm{H}_{2}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: In a 1 L vessel, 10 moles of methane and 50 moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ are present. The number of moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, water and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ present in the vessel are respectively after the vessel was heated to burn methane completely.
explanation:
The combustion reaction for methane is as follows :
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957646/preptracker/questions/q_103372_explanation_1764957644579.png
$\because$ According to balanced equation, ratio of moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ are 2:2:1 respectively.
Hence, final moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=30$ moles ;
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=20$ moles $; \mathrm{CO}_{2}=10$ moles.
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: 28 g KOH is required to completely neutralise $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ produced on heating 60 g of impure $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$. The percentage purity of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is approximately (molar masses of KOH and $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ are 56 and $100 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, respectively)
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{\text{KOH}} + }&{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}&{ \to \,{{\text{K}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \\ {2 \times 56\,{\text{g}}}&{44\,{\text{g}}}&{} \\ {28\,{\text{g}}}&?&{} \end{array}$
$ = \frac{{28 \times 44}}{{2 \times 56}} = 11\,{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_3}}&{\xrightarrow{\Delta }}&{{\text{CaO}} + {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \\ {100\,{\text{g}}}&{}&{44\,{\text{g}}} \\ ?&{}&{11\,{\text{g}}} \end{array}$
$ = \frac{{11 \times 100}}{{44}} = 25{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_3}$
60 g limestone has $25{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_3}$
100 g limestone has?
$\frac{{100 \times 25}}{{60}} = 41.6{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_3}$
$\%$ purity of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=\dfrac{41.6}{100} \times 100=41.6 \%$
subject: Chemistry

question: When 10 g of $90 \%$ pure limestone is heated, the approximate volume (in L ) of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ liberated at STP is
explanation:
Molar mass of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=100 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
10 g of $100 \%$ pure limestone $ = 10\,{\text{g}}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
But 10 g of $90 \%$ pure limestone $=9 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}&{\xrightarrow{\Delta }{\text{CaO}} + }&{{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}} \\ {{\text{1}}\,{\text{mol}}}&{}&{{\text{1}}\,{\text{mol}}} \\ {{\text{100}}\,{\text{g}}}&{}&\begin{gathered} 22.4\,{\text{L}} \hfill \\ {\text{at}}\,\,{\text{STP}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \\ {9\,{\text{g}}}&{}&? \end{array}$
Volume of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ liberated $=\dfrac{9 \times 22.4}{100}=2 \mathrm{~L}$ at STP.
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ reacts with HCl to produce $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. The approximate mass (in g) of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl is (atomic mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=$ 40, $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5$ and $\mathrm{H}=1 \mathrm{gmol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
25 mL of $0.75{\text{M}}\,{\text{HCl}} = 0.01875$ mole of ${\text{HCl}}{\text{.}}$
2 mole HCl reacts with 100 g ( 1 mole) $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ 0.01875 mole HCl reacts with
$=\dfrac{0.01875 \times 100}{2}=0.9375 \approx 0.94$ of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Calculate the approximate mass (in g) of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ required for the following reaction when 15 L of oxygen at STP reacts completely.
$\mathrm{xH}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{yO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{aSO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{bH}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
(molar mass of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}=34 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}} + }&{3{{\text{O}}_2} \to }&{2\,{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_2}}&{ + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \\ {2\,{\text{moles}}}&{3\,{\text{moles}}}&{}&{} \\ {2 \times 34{\kern 1pt} {\text{g}}}&{3 \times 22.4\,{\text{L}}\,\,{\text{at}}\,\,{\text{STP}}}&{}&{} \end{array}$
$3 \times 22.4\,{\text{L}}$ $\,{{\text{O}}_2}$ at STP reacts with $2 \times 34{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}$
$15\,{\text{L}}$ $\,{{\text{O}}_2}$ at STP reacts with
$ = \frac{{15 \times 2 \times 34}}{{3 \times 22.4}} = 15.18{\text{g}}\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
A sample of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}$ weighed 2.21 g is ignited to constant weight of 1.152 g . The composition of mixture is
(Given molar mass in $\mathrm{gmol}^{-1} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=100$,$\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}=84$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$100 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ produces 56 g CaO
$\mathrm{x}\, \mathrm{g} \,\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ produces $\dfrac{56 \mathrm{x}}{100} \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{CaO}$
$\mathrm{MgCO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$84 \mathrm{~g}\, \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}$ produces 40 g MgO
$(2.21-x) \mathrm{g} \mathrm{MgCO}_{3}$ produces
$\dfrac{40(2.21-x)}{84} \,\mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{MgO}$
$\dfrac{56 \mathrm{x}}{100}+\dfrac{(2.21-\mathrm{x}) 40}{84}=1.152 \,\mathrm{~g}$
$\dfrac{56 \mathrm{x}}{100}+\dfrac{88.4-40 \mathrm{x}}{84}=1.152$
On solving, $\mathrm{x}=1.187 \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right.$ mass $)$
$\therefore$ Mass of $\mathrm{MgCO}_{3}=2.21-1.187=1.023 \mathrm{~g}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question:
Combustion of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ produces $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and water. The amount of oxygen (in g ) required for the complete combustion of 900 g of glucose is
[Molar mass of glucose in $\mathrm{g} \,\mathrm{mol}^{-1}=180$ ]

question: The number of moles of methane required to produce $11 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ after complete combustion is (Given molar mass of methane in $\mathrm{gmol}^{-1}: 16$ )
explanation:
$\mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}}\limits_{\scriptstyle{\rm{1 mol }}\atop \scriptstyle{\rm{ or 16\,g}}} {\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \to \mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}\limits_{\scriptstyle{\rm{ 11mol }}\atop \scriptstyle{\rm{or 44\,g}}} {\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
${\rm{44\,g}}\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{is}}\,{\rm{obtained}}\,{\rm{by1\,mole}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$
${\rm{11\,g}}\,\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{is}}\,{\rm{obtained}}\,{\rm{by}}\frac{{{\rm{11 \times 1}}}}{{{\rm{44}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} $
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ = 0}}.{\rm{25}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{mole}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$
${\rm{So,the}}\,{\rm{correct}}\,{\rm{option}}\,{\rm{is(3)}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
If ${\rm{5\,g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is mixed with 14 g of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}$ for the following reaction:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$
At the end, mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$ left unreacted is ____ g.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=\dfrac{5}{2}=2.5 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\dfrac{14}{28}=0.5 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$
1 mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}$ reacts with 3 moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$
0.5 mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}$ reacts with 1.5 moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$
${\mathrm{\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}$ unreacted ${\mathrm{=2.5-1.5=1 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
${\rm{1\,mol}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 2\,g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation: $4\left(\mathrm{FeO} \cdot \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)+8 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+7 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow$$8 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+8 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$$\therefore {\text{l}} = 4;{\text{m}} = 8;{\text{n}} = 7;{\text{x}} = 8,{\text{y}} = 2{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{and}}\,{\text{z}} = 8$

explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}+4 \mathrm{KCl}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$$+6 \mathrm{KHSO}_{4}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

question: When 30 litres of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and 30 litre of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ are reacted $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is formed and the yield is only $50 \%$. The composition of the gaseous mixture will be :
explanation:
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}){\rm{ + 3}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}({\rm{g}}) \to {\rm{2N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}({\rm{g}});$
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is the limiting reagent in this reaction. 10L ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ will react with 30L ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to produce 20L of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$. The composition of resultant mixture will be 5L of ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$, 15L of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 10L of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$.

question: ${\rm{100\,mL}}\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{P}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ on heating forms $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$, the volume change in the reaction is
explanation:
${2\mathop {{\text{P}}{{\text{H}}_3}({\text{g}})}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{2}}\:{\text{mol}}} \\ {{\text{100}}\:{\text{mL}}} \end{array}} \to 2{\text{P}}({\text{s}}) + \mathop {3{{\text{H}}_2}(\,{\text{g}})}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{3}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{mol}}} \\ {{\text{150}}\:{\text{mL}}} \end{array}} }$
$\therefore $There is an increase of 150-100=50mL

question: The mass of oxygen that would be required to produce enough $\mathrm{CO}$ which completely reduces ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.6kg}}\,\,{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ (atomic mass of $\mathrm{Fe}=56$ ), is
explanation:
$3{\rm{C}} + \frac{3}{2}{{\rm{O}}_2} \to 3{\rm{CO}}$
${\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_2}{{\rm{O}}_3} + 3{\rm{CO}} \to 2{\rm{Fe}} + 3{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_2}$
$\because $ 1 mole of
${{\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}} \equiv 3{\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{CO}} \equiv \frac{3}{2}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{160}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}\,{\rm{require}}$
${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\,\frac{{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 32}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{48}}\,{\rm{g}}$
${\text{Thus,1}}{\text{.6}}\,{\text{Kg}}\,{\text{(or1600g)}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{will require }}{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$
${\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{480}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What volume of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ will be liberated at $\mathrm{NTP}$, if $12 \mathrm{~g}$ of carbon is burnt in excess of oxygen?
explanation:
${\mathop {{\text{C}}({\text{g}})}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{1mol}}} \\ {{\text{12g}}} \end{array}} + {{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}}) \to \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}(\,{\text{g}})}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{1mol}}} \\ {{\text{22}}.{\text{4L}}} \end{array}} }$
$\therefore $ If 12g of carbon is burnt in excess of oxygen, $22.4\,{\rm{L}}\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ will be liberated.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which statement is false for the balanced equation given below?
$\mathrm{CS}_{2}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
explanation:
3 moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ react with 1 mole of $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$.
So, 6 moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ react with 2 moles of $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$.

options.text: One mole of $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ will produce one mole of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
The reaction of 16 g of oxygen produces 7.33 g of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
The reaction of one mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will produce $2 / 3$ mole of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$.
Six molecules of oxygen requires three molecules of $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$.

subject: Chemistry

question: 1.12 mL of a gas is produced at STP by the action of 4.12 mg of alcohol, with methyl magnesium iodide. The molecular mass of alcohol is
explanation:
$\mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{MgROI}$
$\therefore 1$ mole alcohol gives 1 mole $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$.
According to data,
1.12 L(at STP) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is obtained from 4.12 g alcohol.
$\therefore 22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ (at STP) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is obtained from
$=\dfrac{22.4 \times 4.12}{1.12}=82.4 \mathrm{~g}$
So, $82.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-}$is the molar mass of alcohol.
subject: Chemistry

question: Calcium carbide produces acetylene on reaction with water, ethylene can be obtained by reduction of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$. Polythene is produced by polymerization of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$. Calculate the theoretical yield of polythene per kilogram of $\mathrm{CaC}_{2}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{CaC}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text { reduction }} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \xrightarrow{\text { Polymerization }}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$
$\Rightarrow 1$ mole $\mathrm{CaC}_{2}$ produces $=1$ mole polythene
$\Rightarrow 64 \mathrm{g\,CaC}_{2}$ produces $=28 \mathrm{~g}$ polythene.
$\Rightarrow 1 \mathrm{~kg}(1000 \mathrm{~g}) \mathrm{CaC}_{2}$
produces $\Rightarrow \dfrac{28}{64} \times 1000 \mathrm{~g}$ polythene
$=437.5 \mathrm{~g}$ polythene (or) 0.4375 kg .
subject: Chemistry

question: In the reaction $4 \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{~B}+3 \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}_{4} \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{3}$, what will be the number of moles of product formed, starting from 1 mol of $\mathrm{A}, 0.6 \mathrm{~mol}$ of B and 0.72 mol of C ?
explanation:
4 moles of A needs 2 moles B
1 mole of A needs ?
$=\dfrac{1 \times 2}{4}=0.5$ mole B
But given 'B' is 0.6 , So, it is excess.
4 moles of A needs 3 moles C
1 moles of A needs?
$=\dfrac{1 \times 3}{4}=0.75$ mole C
But given is 0.72 mole. So 'C' is limiting reagent.
3 mole ' C ' is gives 1 mole Product
0.72 mole ' C ' gives?
$=0.72 \times \dfrac{1}{3}=0.24$ mole product
subject: Chemistry

question:
446 g of PbO, 46 g of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 16 g of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
are allowed to react according to the equation $\mathrm{PbO}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$. The amount of $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ that can be produced is
(At. wt. of $\mathrm{Pb}=207$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{PbO}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{PbO}}=\dfrac{446}{(207+16)}=\dfrac{446}{223}=2$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}=\dfrac{46}{46}=1$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\dfrac{16}{32}=0.5$
1 mole PbO needs 2 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
$\therefore 2$ moles PbO needs ?
$=\dfrac{2 \times 2}{1}=4$ mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
So, PbO is excess
2 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ needs $\dfrac{1}{2}$ mole $\mathrm{O}_{2}$
1 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ needs ?
$ = \frac{{2 \times 2}}{1} = 4\,\,{\rm{mole}}\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
So, PbO is excess
2 mole ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ needs $\frac{1}{2}\,\,{\rm{mole}}\,\,{{\rm{O}}_2}$
1 mole ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ needs ?
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = 0.25\,\,{\rm{mole}}\,\,{{\rm{O}}_2}$
but given is 0.5 mole $\mathrm{O}_{2}$, So It is excess.
$\therefore \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is limiting reagent.
2 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ forms $=1 \mathrm{~mole} \mathrm{~Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
1 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ forms $=0.5$ mole $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
$\mathrm{wt}_{\mathrm{PbNO}_{3}}=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{MM}$
$=0.5 \times 331=165.5 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $100\,{\rm{mL}}$ of ${\rm{P}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ when completely decomposed produced phosphorus and hydrogen. The change in volume of the gas is $\mathrm{PH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{s})+3 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{PH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{s})+\dfrac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
1 mole gas changes into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ moles
So, 100 mL will convert to$\Rightarrow 100 \times 3=150 \mathrm{~mL}$
$\therefore$ Change in volume $=150-100=50 \mathrm{~mL}$ increase.
subject: Chemistry

question: Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the given options given.
explanation:
$(1)\mathop {2{{\rm{H}}_2}}\limits_{1.0{\rm{g}}} + \mathop {{{\rm{O}}_2}}\limits_{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.0g}}} \to 2{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{2}{\rm{mol}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{\,32}}\,{\rm{mol}}$
${{\text{O}}_2}$ is the limiting reagent :
$1\,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{O}}_2} = 2\,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{32}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{O}}_2} \equiv \frac{1}{{16}}\,{\text{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
$ = \frac{{18}}{{16}}\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} = 1.125\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
$(2)\mathop {{{\rm{N}}_2}}\limits_{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.0g}}} + \mathop {3{{\rm{H}}_2}}\limits_{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.0}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}} \to 2{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{28}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{2}\,\,{\rm{mol}}$
${{\text{N}}_2}$ is the limiting reagent.
$\therefore \frac{1}{{28}}\,{\rm{mol}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}} \equiv \frac{1}{{14}}\,{\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} $
${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}} \equiv \frac{1}{{14}} \times 17\,{\rm{g}} = 1.214{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{g}}$
$(3)\,\,\mathop {{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{1}}{\text{.0}}\,\,{\text{g}}} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {{\text{CaO}}}\limits_{\text{?}} + {\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$
$\frac{1}{{100}}\,\,\,{\text{mol}}\,\,\,\,{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$
$\frac{1}{{100}} \times 56\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{CaO}} = 0.56\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{CaO}}$
$(4){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \mathop {\rm{C}}\limits_{1.0{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}} + \mathop {{\rm{2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}\limits_{1.0{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{12}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{1}{2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}$
C is the limiting reagent.
$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{{12}}\,\,\,\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{C}} \equiv \frac{1}{{12}}\,\,{\text{mol}}$
$\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}} \equiv \frac{{16}}{{12}}\,\,\,{\text{g}} = 1.333\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In the formation reaction of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ from $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}, 140 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $40\,{\rm{g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_2}$ were mixed.Select the option which is correct.
explanation:
$\underset{28 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{~N}_{2}}+\underset{6 \mathrm{~g}}{3 \mathrm{H}_{2}} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
$28 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~N}_{2}$ needs $6\,{\rm{g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_2}$
$140\,{\rm{g}}\,{{\rm{N}}_2}$ needs ?
$ = \frac{{146 \times 6}}{{28}} = 30{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{g}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
But given is $40{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{g}}\,\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
So, ${{\text{N}}_2}$ is limiting reagent.
$28\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{N}}_2}$ gives $2 \times 17\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{g}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}$
$140\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{N}}_2}$ gives ?
$ = \frac{{140 \times 2 \times 17}}{{28}} = 170\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}$ is expected
If 85 gram ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}$ is actually formed.
$\% {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{yield}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = \frac{{85}}{{170}} \times 100 = \frac{{100}}{2} = 50\% $

options.text: Maximum mass of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ which can be formed is 180 g .
If $\% $ yield of reaction is 80 , then $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ consumed will be 32 g .
Some $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ will be left after the reaction.
If $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ formed is 85 g then $\% $ yield will be $50 \%$.

subject: Chemistry

question: According to following reaction,$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{BO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ the number of moles of $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ produced if 1 mole of A is mixed with 1 mole of $\mathrm{BO}_{3}$ is
explanation:
$9 \mathrm{~A}+8 \mathrm{BO}_{3} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~A}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+4 \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
9 moles of A needs 8 moles of B
1 mole of A needs?
$=1 \times \dfrac{8}{9}=0.89$ mole B
But given ' B ' is 1 mole
So, ' A ' is limiting reagnet
9 moles of A form 3 moles of $\mathrm{A}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$.
So, 1 mole of A form?
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{1 \times 3}}{9} = \frac{1}{3}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mole}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{A}}_{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many moles of $\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{FeS}_{2}\right)$ can be made from 2 moles of zinc, 3 moles of iron, and 5 moles of sulphur?
explanation:
1 mole of $\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{FeS}_{2}\right)$ contains: 1 mole of Zn , 1 mole of $\mathrm{Fe}, 2$ moles of S .
So, for 2 moles of $\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{FeS}_{2}\right)$, we need 2 moles of $\mathrm{Zn}, 2$ moles of Fe , and 4 moles of S .
So maximum 2 moles of $\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{FeS}_{2}\right)$ can be made. (1mole 'Fe' 1mole ' S ' will be unreacted).
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: A reactant that is entirely consumed when a reaction goes to completion is known as limiting reactant.


Reason: The amount of limiting reactant limits the amount of product formed.
explanation: The amount of product in a chemical reaction is dictated by the limiting reagent, the reactant fully consumed first, setting the maximum achievable product quantity. Whereas other reactants in excess don't impact the upper limit on product yield. So, the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: The composition of residual mixture will be, if $\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}}$ combines with $\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}}$
$\mathrm{2 \times 24 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}}$ reacts with $\mathrm{32 \mathrm{~g}}$ oxygen
$\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}}$ reacts with
$\mathrm{\dfrac{32}{2 \times 24} \times 30=20 \mathrm{gO}_{2}}$
So, $\mathrm{10 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_{2}}$ will be left unreacted and
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}}$ is the limiting reagent
$\mathrm{2 \times 24 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}}$ forms $\mathrm{2 \times 40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{MgO}}$
$\mathrm{30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}}$ forms $\mathrm{\mathrm{MgO}}$
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{2 \times 40}{2 \times 24} \times 30=50 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{MgO}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What mass of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}$ in grams would be enough to produce $\mathrm{14.35 \mathrm{gm}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}}$ when it reacts with $\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{\underset{111 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\underset{2 \times 143.5 \mathrm{~g}}{2 \mathrm{AgCl}}}$
$\mathrm{111 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}$ is required to produce $\mathrm{2 \times 143.5}$ of$\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}$ required to produce $\mathrm{14.35 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}}$
$\rm{=\dfrac{111 \times 14.35}{2 \times 143.5}=5.55 \mathrm{~g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{X}$ ' litres of carbon monoxide is present at STP. It is completely oxidized to $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$. The volume of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ formed is 11.207 litres at STP. What is the value of ' $\mathrm{\mathrm{X}}$ ' in litres?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}}$ gives 1 mole of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore 11.207 \mathrm{~L}}$ of CO at STP gives $\mathrm{11.207 \mathrm{~L}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}$ at STP.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the amount of water formed by combustion of $\mathrm{1.6 \mathrm{~g}}$ methane ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\underset{\substack{\text { 1mole } \\ \text { or } 16 \mathrm{gg}}}{\mathrm{CH}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\underset{\substack{2 \text { molos } \\ \text { or } 36 \mathrm{~g}}}{2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(1)}}}$
$\mathrm{16 \mathrm{~g}}$ methane on combustion produces $\mathrm{36 \mathrm{~g}}$ water.
$\mathrm{\therefore 1.6 \mathrm{~g}}$ methane on combustion produces ? g water.
$\rm{=\dfrac{1.6 \times 36}{16}=3.6 \mathrm{~g} \text { water. }}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is the mass of potassium chloride produced when $\mathrm{12.25 \mathrm{~g}}$ potassium chlorate undergoes decomposition? (At. mass
$\mathrm{\mathrm{K}=39, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{O}=16}$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{KClO}_{3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{KCl}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}}$$\mathrm{2 \times 122.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{KClO}_{3}}$ gives $\mathrm{2 \times 74.5 \mathrm{~g} \times \mathrm{KCl}}$
$\mathrm{12.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{KClO}_{3}}$ gives?
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{12.25 \times 2 \times 74.5}{2 \times 122.5}=7.45 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{KCl}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What amount of oxygen is used at S.T.P. to obtain $\mathrm{9 \mathrm{~g}}$ water from sufficient amount of hydrogen gas?
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)}}$
$\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ mole oxygen means $\mathrm{11.2 \mathrm{~L}}$ (or) $\mathrm{\mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ at STP
Here, $\mathrm{11.2 \mathrm{dm}^{3}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ gives $\mathrm{18 \mathrm{~g}}$ water (at STP)
$\mathrm{\therefore 9 \mathrm{~g}}$ water is obtained from
$\frac{{{\rm{11}}{\rm{.2}} \times {\rm{9}}}}{{{\rm{18}}}}{\rm{ = 5}}{\rm{.6d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ of }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{3.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ of mixture of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ were dissolved and made up to $\mathrm{250 \mathrm{~mL} .25 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of this solution were completely neutralised by $\mathrm{17 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{(\mathrm{N} / 2) \mathrm{HCl}}$ solution. Then, the percentage of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ in mixture is
explanation:
Suppose the amount of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ in the mixture $\mathrm{=\mathrm{xg}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Amount of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}=(3.5-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}}$
$\mathrm{25 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of alkali solution $\mathrm{=17 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{(\mathrm{N} / 2) \mathrm{HCl}}$
or $\mathrm{250 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of alkali solution $\mathrm{=170 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{(\mathrm{N} / 2) \mathrm{HCl}}$
$\mathrm{1000 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{(\mathrm{N}) \mathrm{HCl}}$ contains $\mathrm{36.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$
$\mathrm{170 \mathrm{~mL}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{N} / 2 \mathrm{HCl}}$ will contain
$\mathrm{\dfrac{36.5 \times 170}{1000 \times 2}=3.1025 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ Eq. mass of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}=40 \mathrm{geq} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$
and that of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}=56{\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{eq} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}^{-1}}$
$\mathrm{40 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ require $\mathrm{36.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$
$\mathrm{\mathrm{xg}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ will require $\mathrm{\dfrac{36.5 \times \mathrm{x}}{40} \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$
Similarly, $\mathrm{56 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ require $\mathrm{36.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$
(3.5-x) of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$ will require
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{36.5(3.5-\mathrm{x})}{56} \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$
Thus, $\mathrm{\dfrac{36.5 \mathrm{x}}{40}+\dfrac{36.5(3.5-\mathrm{x})}{56}=3.1025 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{HCl}}$ or $\mathrm{\mathrm{x}=3.15 \mathrm{~g}}$
$\mathrm{=}$ Amount of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ in the mixture $\mathrm{\therefore}$ Amount of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}=(3.5-3.15) \mathrm{g}=0.35 \mathrm{~g}}$
$\mathrm{3.5 \mathrm{~g}}$ of the mixture contain $\mathrm{0.35 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$
$\mathrm{100 \mathrm{~g}}$ of the mixture contain $\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.35 \mathrm{~g} \times 100}{3.5}}$
$\mathrm{=10 \mathrm{~g}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}}$
Hence, $\mathrm{\%}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{KOH}=10 \%}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The right option for the mass of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ produced by heating $20 \mathrm{~g}$ of $20 \%$ pure limestone is (Atomic mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=40$ )
$\left[ {{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{1200 K}}}}{\text{CaO + C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]$
explanation:
Weight of impure limestone $=20 \mathrm{~g}$
Weight of pure limestone $\left(\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}\right)=20 \%$ of $20 \mathrm{~g}$
$=\dfrac{20}{100} \times 20=4 \mathrm{~g}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953053/preptracker/questions/q_15228_explanation_1764953053067.png $=\dfrac{4 \times 44}{100}=1.76 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 8 litre of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and 6 litre of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$ are allowed to react to maximum possible extent. Find out the final volume of reaction mixture. Assume pressure and temperature remains constant throughout the course of reaction
explanation:
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}} + {\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}} \to 2HCl$
${\rm{Volume}}\,\,{\rm{before}}\,\,{\rm{reaction}}\,\,{\rm{8L,}}\,\,{\rm{6L,}}\,\,{\rm{0}}$
${\rm{Volume}}\,\,{\rm{after}}\,\,{\rm{reaction}}\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{2L,}}\,\,{\rm{0L,}}\,\,{\rm{12L}}$
$\therefore $ Volume after reaction
= Volume of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$ left + Volume of HCl formed
= 2 + 12 = 14 L.
subject: Chemistry

question: When 1.25 gm of a chalk sample $\left( {{\rm{CaC}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)$ is strongly heated, 0.44 gm ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ gas is produced. Determine the percentage yield of the reaction.
subject: Chemistry

question: A silver coin weighing 11.34 g was dissolved in nitric acid. When sodium chloride was added to the solution all the silver (present as ${\rm{AgN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$) was precipitated as silver chloride. The weight of the precipitated silver chloride 14.35 g. Calculate the percentage of silver in the coin
explanation:
${\rm{Ag + 2HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}} \to {\rm{AgN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ + N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
${\rm{AgN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ + NaCl}} \to {\rm{AgCl + NaN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$
$\therefore $ 143.5 gm of silver chloride would be precipitated by 108 g of silver.
14.35 g of silver chloride would be precipitated 10.8 g of silver.
11.34 g of silver coin contain 10.8 g of pure silver.
$\therefore $ 100 g of silver coin contain $\frac{{{\rm{10}}{\rm{.8}}}}{{{\rm{11}}{\rm{.34}}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}$
= 95.2% of pure silver
subject: Chemistry

question: 25 kg of ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$ and 5 kg of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$ are mixed to produce ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(g)}}$. The limiting reagent in the production of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is
explanation:
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ + }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}} \to {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{(g)}}$
Mass ${\rm{25}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{5}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}$
Moles: $\frac{{{\rm{25 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}}{{{\rm{28}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.893 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{\rm{5 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.5 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}$
According to the equation, 1 mol ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$ requires 3 mol ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$, for the reaction.
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.893 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}$ mol requires
${\rm{3 \times 0}}{\rm{.893 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol = 2}}{\rm{.68 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}$
But we have only ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.5 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}$ mol of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$. Hence, dihydrogen is the limiting reagent in this case.
subject: Chemistry

question: 2.76 g of silver carbonate on being strongly heated yield a residue weighing
explanation:
(I) Mole method:
${\text{2A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{4Ag + 2C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}$
$\,\,\,{\rm{2}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{4}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}$
${\rm{2 \times 276}}\,\,{\rm{g}}\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{4 \times 108}}\,\,{\rm{g}}$${\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}$
${\rm{2 \times 276}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4 \times 108}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
${\rm{2 \times 276}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{o}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{gives}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4 \times 108}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Ag}}$
${\rm{2}}{\rm{.76}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{will}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{give}}$
$\left( {\frac{{{\rm{4 \times 108 \times 2}}{\rm{.76}}}}{{{\rm{2 \times 276}}}}} \right){\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.16}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Ag}}{\rm{.}}$
(II) Principle of Atomic Conservation (POAC) method:
Moles of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ = Total moles of Ag as product
2 moles of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ = 4 moles of Ag
${\rm{2 \times 276}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$ of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ = ${\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4 \times 108}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$ of Ag
2.76 g of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} $ ${\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{4 \times 108 \times 2}}{\rm{.76}}}}{{{\rm{2 \times 276}}}}} \right){\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.16}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Ag}}{\rm{.}}$
(III) Equivalent Method:
Number of gram - equivalents of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\mkern 1mu} $ = Number of gram-equivalents of Ag
$\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.76}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\rm{2 \times 276}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g/mol}}}}{{\rm{1}}}} \right)}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{yg}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\rm{4 \times 108g/mol}}}}{{\rm{1}}}} \right)}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{y = 2}}{\rm{.16g}}$
[Change in oxidation state of ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{g}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ and Ag is 1, respectively]
subject: Chemistry

question: Two litre of oxygen was reacted with five litre of CO at STP. The limiting reagent and mass of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ produced are, respectively
explanation:
${\rm{2CO(g) + }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}} \to {\rm{2C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$
Initially, 5L, 2L
2 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ combines with 4 L of CO. Hence, ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is the limiting reagent.
When 2 L of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ combines with 4 L of CO, 4 L of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is formed.
22.4 L of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ has the mass of 44 g
4 L of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ has the mass of $\frac{{{\rm{4 \times 44}}}}{{{\rm{22}}{\rm{.4}}}}{\rm{ = 7}}{\rm{.86}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 40 g of a sample of carbon on combustion left 10% of it unreacted. The volume of oxygen required at STP for this combustion reaction is

$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{12}}\,\,{\rm{g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{22}}{\rm{.4}}\,\,{\rm{L}}$
Mass volume relationship for the chemical equation,
Step 2:
${\rm{C(s)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ + }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \to {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$

40 g
Step 3: Calculate the limiting reagent.
As 10%, i.e., $\left( {{\rm{40 \times }}\frac{{{\rm{10}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ = 4g}}} \right)$ of sample of carbon left unreacted. Hence, ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ acts as a limiting agent.
Step 4: Calculate the volume of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ at STP

${\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{12}}{\rm{.4}}}}{{{\rm{4 \times 31}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}$

question:
What weight of ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is needed to convert 62 gm of ${{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ in the reaction ?
${{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}} \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
explanation:
The equivalent weight of ${{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{31 \times 4}}}}{{{\rm{5 \times 4}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{31}}}}{{\rm{5}}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{62}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{gm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{62 \times 5}}}}{{{\rm{31}}}}{\rm{equivalents}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}$
${\rm{ = 10}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{equivalents}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{4}}}$
The equivalent weight of ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Mol}}{\rm{.wt}}}}{{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{63}}}}{{\rm{1}}}$
$\therefore $The weight of ${\rm{HN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ required ${\rm{ = 10 \times 63 = 630gm}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
250 mg of a compound react with ${\rm{AgN}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$ produces 141 mg of silver bromide. The percentage of bromine in the compound is:
(at. mass Ag = 108; Br = 80 )
explanation:
Mass of substance = 250mg = 0.250g
Mass of AgBr = 141mg = 0.141g
1 mole of AgBr = 1g atom of Br
188 g of AgBr = 80g of Br
$\therefore $ 188 g of AgBr contain bromine = 80 g
0.141 g of AgBr contain bromine = $\frac{{{\rm{80}}}}{{{\rm{188}}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.141}}$
This much amount of bromine present in 0.250 g of compound
$\therefore \,\,\,{\rm{\% }}\,{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{bromine = }}\frac{{{\rm{80}}}}{{{\rm{188}}}}{\rm{ \times }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.414}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.250}}}}{\rm{ \times 100 = 24\% }}$
subject: Chemistry

${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$

${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{moles}}$
